person.name: Albert Einstein
person.fact: Developed the theory of relativity; famously failed his university entrance exam on the first try

person.name: Marie Curie
person.fact: First person to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences: Physics and Chemistry

person.name: Isaac Newton
person.fact: Famously inspired by a falling apple; invented calculus simultaneously with Leibniz

person.name: Charles Darwin
person.fact: Spent five years aboard HMS Beagle before proposing natural selection

person.name: Nikola Tesla
person.fact: Held over 300 patents and had a famous rivalry with Edison over AC vs DC power

person.name: Thomas Edison
person.fact: Held over 1,000 US patents and said genius is '1% inspiration, 99% perspiration'

person.name: Stephen Hawking
person.fact: Communicated via a speech synthesiser after losing his voice; wrote A Brief History of Time

person.name: Ada Lovelace
person.fact: Wrote the world's first algorithm, designed for a machine that hadn't been built yet

person.name: Napoleon Bonaparte
person.fact: Was notably NOT short by the standards of his era; 5'7" was average height

person.name: Cleopatra
person.fact: Spoke nine languages and was the first Ptolemaic ruler to actually learn Egyptian

person.name: Julius Caesar
person.fact: Reportedly cried 'Et tu, Brute?' upon seeing his trusted friend among his assassins

person.name: Genghis Khan
person.fact: United warring Mongol tribes and created the largest contiguous land empire in history

person.name: Joan of Arc
person.fact: Led French armies to key victories at age 17 before being burned at the stake at 19

person.name: Leonardo da Vinci
person.fact: Filled over 13,000 pages of notebooks with ideas ranging from flying machines to anatomy

person.name: Michelangelo
person.fact: Painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling lying on his back over four years, finishing in 1512

person.name: William Shakespeare
person.fact: Invented over 1,700 words still used in English, including 'bedroom' and 'lonely'

person.name: Pablo Picasso
person.fact: Co-founded Cubism and was so prolific he produced an estimated 20,000 works

person.name: Vincent van Gogh
person.fact: Sold only one painting in his lifetime, famously cut off his own ear, and his work now fetches hundreds of millions

person.name: Frida Kahlo
person.fact: Painted 55 self-portraits, many depicting pain from a near-fatal bus accident at 18

person.name: Edgar Allan Poe
person.fact: Married his 13-year-old cousin and died mysteriously at 40, found delirious in someone else's clothes

person.name: Mark Twain
person.fact: Born and died in years when Halley's Comet appeared; predicted his own death accordingly

person.name: Jane Austen
person.fact: Published her novels anonymously as 'By a Lady'; never married despite writing about it endlessly

person.name: Ernest Hemingway
person.fact: Survived two plane crashes in two days, then read his own obituaries over breakfast

person.name: Abraham Lincoln
person.fact: The tallest US president at 6'4"; also a licensed bartender and skilled wrestler

person.name: Winston Churchill
person.fact: Delivered the 'We shall fight on the beaches' speech and won the Nobel Prize in Literature

person.name: Nelson Mandela
person.fact: Served 27 years in prison before becoming South Africa's first democratically elected president

person.name: Mahatma Gandhi
person.fact: Led a 240-mile march to the sea to protest the British salt tax in 1930

person.name: George Washington
person.fact: Refused a salary as president and declined a third term, setting the two-term tradition

person.name: Queen Victoria
person.fact: Reigned for 63 years and had nine children who married into royal families across Europe

person.name: Franklin D. Roosevelt
person.fact: Only US president elected four times; led the nation through both the Depression and WWII

person.name: Muhammad Ali
person.fact: Refused military draft saying 'No Viet Cong ever called me n*****'; stripped of his title

person.name: Pelé
person.fact: Only player to win three FIFA World Cups and scored a record 1,281 goals in his career

person.name: Serena Williams
person.fact: Won the 2017 Australian Open while eight weeks pregnant, her 23rd Grand Slam title

person.name: Michael Jordan
person.fact: His Nike shoe deal launched one of the most iconic and lucrative sportswear brands in history

person.name: Usain Bolt
person.fact: Set the 100m world record of 9.58 seconds with a shoelace untied at Berlin 2009

person.name: Jesse Owens
person.fact: Won four gold medals at the 1936 Berlin Olympics under Hitler's gaze

person.name: Babe Ruth
person.fact: Hit 714 home runs and also pitched so well the Yankees didn't want to waste him on the mound

person.name: Elvis Presley
person.fact: His hip-swivelling TV appearances were considered too scandalous to film below the waist

person.name: Marilyn Monroe
person.fact: Sang 'Happy Birthday, Mr. President' to JFK in a skin-tight dress at Madison Square Garden

person.name: Charlie Chaplin
person.fact: Was once rejected in his own lookalike contest; he entered for a joke

person.name: Frank Sinatra
person.fact: Known as 'Ol' Blue Eyes'; his FBI file ran to 1,275 pages

person.name: Freddie Mercury
person.fact: Wrote 'Bohemian Rhapsody' in one sitting; the band nearly ditched it as too long

person.name: David Bowie
person.fact: Created at least 10 distinct alter egos, starting with Ziggy Stardust in 1972

person.name: Audrey Hepburn
person.fact: Spent her later years as a UNICEF Goodwill Ambassador, traveling to conflict zones across Africa, South America, and Asia to advocate for children

person.name: Alfred Hitchcock
person.fact: Made a cameo appearance in almost every one of his 50+ films

person.name: Walt Disney
person.fact: Was fired from a newspaper for 'lacking imagination'; went on to build the world's most famous entertainment empire

person.name: Sigmund Freud
person.fact: Smoked 20 cigars a day and reportedly said 'Sometimes a cigar is just a cigar'

person.name: Karl Marx
person.fact: Spent most of his life broke and in exile, repeatedly writing to his closest collaborator to beg for money

person.name: Coco Chanel
person.fact: Liberated women from corsets and made the little black dress a wardrobe staple

person.name: Amelia Earhart
person.fact: First woman to fly solo across the Atlantic; disappeared attempting a round-the-world flight in 1937

person.name: Galileo Galilei
person.fact: Was placed under house arrest for the rest of his life for supporting a heliocentric solar system

person.name: Alan Turing
person.fact: Cracked the Nazi Enigma code, credited with shortening WWII by two years

person.name: Richard Feynman
person.fact: Decoded the Challenger disaster cause by dipping an O-ring in ice water at the live hearing

person.name: Carl Sagan
person.fact: Wrote the message on the Voyager Golden Record and coined the phrase 'billions and billions'

person.name: Louis Pasteur
person.fact: Invented pasteurisation and created the first vaccines for rabies and anthrax

person.name: Alexander Fleming
person.fact: Discovered penicillin by accident when mould contaminated a petri dish he'd left before holiday

person.name: Rosalind Franklin
person.fact: Her Photo 51 X-ray image was the key evidence that revealed DNA's double-helix structure

person.name: Gregor Mendel
person.fact: Grew and counted over 10,000 pea plants in a monastery garden to discover heredity

person.name: Edwin Hubble
person.fact: Proved the universe is expanding; the space telescope launched in 1990 bears his name

person.name: Niels Bohr
person.fact: His model of the atom, electrons orbiting a nucleus like planets around the sun, became the iconic image of the atomic age

person.name: Archimedes
person.fact: Allegedly ran naked through Syracuse shouting 'Eureka!' after discovering water displacement in his bath

person.name: Nicolaus Copernicus
person.fact: A Catholic canon who quietly spent decades proving the Church's own cosmological teachings wrong

person.name: James Watt
person.fact: The unit of power is named after him: your phone charger, your lightbulb, your microwave are all rated in his name

person.name: Lise Meitner
person.fact: Co-discovered nuclear fission but was excluded from the Nobel Prize given to her male colleague

person.name: Max Planck
person.fact: Founded quantum theory but spent years trying to disprove it; the fundamental unit of quantum action is named after him

person.name: Ernest Rutherford
person.fact: Discovered the atomic nucleus by firing particles at gold foil and watching some bounce back

person.name: Michael Faraday
person.fact: Had almost no formal education and couldn't do higher mathematics; invented the electric motor anyway

person.name: Neil Armstrong
person.fact: Flubbed his planned 'one small step' line slightly, but billions heard it as written anyway

person.name: Werner Heisenberg
person.fact: Gave his name to the Uncertainty Principle, stating you can't know both position and speed of a particle

person.name: James Clerk Maxwell
person.fact: United electricity, magnetism, and light into four equations that Einstein called the greatest achievement in physics

person.name: Alexander the Great
person.fact: Was never defeated in battle and had conquered most of the known world by age 30

person.name: Attila the Hun
person.fact: Called 'The Scourge of God'; turned back from sacking Rome partly due to a meeting with Pope Leo I

person.name: Marco Polo
person.fact: Spent 17 years at the court of Kublai Khan, dictated his travels while in prison, and has a popular swimming pool game named after him

person.name: Christopher Columbus
person.fact: Died still believing he had reached Asia; never knew he had landed in the Americas

person.name: Catherine the Great
person.fact: Ruled Russia for 34 years, expanded its territory massively, and corresponded regularly with Voltaire

person.name: Queen Elizabeth I
person.fact: The 'Virgin Queen' who defeated the Spanish Armada in 1588 and never married

person.name: Queen Elizabeth II
person.fact: Longest-reigning British monarch at 70 years, and the first female member of the royal family to serve in the military

person.name: Martin Luther
person.fact: Nailed 95 theses to a church door in 1517, sparking the Protestant schism that permanently divided Western Christianity

person.name: Che Guevara
person.fact: His iconic beret photo became the most reproduced image in history; he was executed in Bolivia aged 39

person.name: Margaret Thatcher
person.fact: The 'Iron Lady' was Britain's first female prime minister and served three consecutive terms

person.name: John F. Kennedy
person.fact: The youngest elected US president; delivered the 'Ich bin ein Berliner' speech in divided Berlin

person.name: Mao Zedong
person.fact: Founded the People's Republic of China in 1949 and initiated the Great Leap Forward

person.name: Joseph Stalin
person.fact: Born with a different name, he adopted a pseudonym meaning 'Man of Steel', and ruled the Soviet Union through it's most brutal regime

person.name: Thomas Jefferson
person.fact: Wrote the Declaration of Independence at 33; also invented the swivel chair and macaroni machine

person.name: Theodore Roosevelt
person.fact: Shot during a campaign speech, then spoke for 90 minutes before seeking medical treatment

person.name: Vladimir Lenin
person.fact: His embalmed body has been on public display in Moscow's Red Square since 1924

person.name: Fidel Castro
person.fact: Survived an estimated 638 assassination attempts, many organised by the CIA

person.name: Simón Bolívar
person.fact: Known as 'El Libertador'; freed six South American countries from Spanish rule

person.name: Martin Luther King Jr.
person.fact: Delivered the 'I Have a Dream' speech to 250,000 people in Washington DC in 1963

person.name: Harriet Tubman
person.fact: Led over 70 enslaved people to freedom via the Underground Railroad and never lost a passenger

person.name: John Lennon
person.fact: Wrote 'Imagine' and was once more famous than Jesus, per his own controversial claim

person.name: Paul McCartney
person.fact: 'Yesterday' is the most covered song in history; he has held the world record for most #1 albums

person.name: Michael Jackson
person.fact: Thriller is the best-selling album of all time; he moonwalked into history in 1983

person.name: Bob Dylan
person.fact: First rock musician to win the Nobel Prize in Literature; his acceptance speech was delivered by Patti Smith

person.name: Jimi Hendrix
person.fact: Self-taught, played guitar left-handed on a right-handed instrument strung upside-down

person.name: Whitney Houston
person.fact: Her version of 'I Will Always Love You' is among the best-selling singles ever recorded

person.name: Madonna
person.fact: Has sold over 300 million records, making her the best-selling female recording artist of all time

person.name: Aretha Franklin
person.fact: The 'Queen of Soul'; first woman inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame

person.name: Bob Marley
person.fact: Reggae's global ambassador; his album Legend is the best-selling reggae album ever made

person.name: Louis Armstrong
person.fact: Known as 'Satchmo'; his gravelly voice on 'What a Wonderful World' became one of the most beloved recordings

person.name: Miles Davis
person.fact: His 1959 album Kind of Blue is the best-selling jazz record in history

person.name: Jim Morrison
person.fact: Front man of The Doors; earned the nickname 'The Lizard King' and died in Paris aged 27

person.name: Kurt Cobain
person.fact: Wrote 'Smells Like Teen Spirit' in 15 minutes and accidentally launched the grunge movement

person.name: Elton John
person.fact: Born plain Reginald Dwight, he reinvented himself as one of music's most flamboyant performers, famous for his outrageous costumes and enormous glasses collection

person.name: Prince
person.fact: Fought his record label by writing 'SLAVE' on his face and changing his name to an unpronounceable symbol

person.name: Tina Turner
person.fact: The 'Queen of Rock and Roll'; made her biggest comeback at age 44 with Private Dancer

person.name: Stevie Wonder
person.fact: Blind since shortly after birth, he became a UN Messenger of Peace and a driving force behind making Martin Luther King Jr. Day a US federal holiday

person.name: Marlon Brando
person.fact: Famously improvised the cat-stroking opening scene of The Godfather on the day of filming

person.name: James Dean
person.fact: Appeared in only three major films before dying in a car crash at 24; became an eternal rebel icon

person.name: Humphrey Bogart
person.fact: 'Here's looking at you, kid', voted the greatest male star in Hollywood history by the AFI

person.name: Grace Kelly
person.fact: Gave up Hollywood stardom to marry Prince Rainier III and become Princess of Monaco

person.name: Cary Grant
person.fact: His debonair screen persona was invented from scratch; born Archibald Leach in a Bristol working-class family

person.name: Orson Welles
person.fact: Directed and starred in Citizen Kane at 25; his War of the Worlds radio broadcast caused public panic in 1938

person.name: Ingrid Bergman
person.fact: Her affair with an Italian director caused such a scandal that she was denounced on the US Senate floor and effectively blacklisted from Hollywood for years

person.name: Clark Gable
person.fact: Known as 'The King of Hollywood'; his refusal to wear an undershirt in It Happened One Night supposedly crashed undershirt sales

person.name: Charles Dickens
person.fact: He based the miserly Ebenezer Scrooge partly on real London slum conditions he witnessed as a child laborer in a boot-blacking factory.

person.name: Leo Tolstoy
person.fact: He wrote one of the longest novels ever published; War and Peace spans over 1,200 pages and tracks five aristocratic families through Napoleon's invasion of Russia.

person.name: Fyodor Dostoevsky
person.fact: He was lined up before a firing squad and pardoned at the last second, an experience that permanently shaped his obsessive exploration of death and suffering in his novels.

person.name: George Orwell
person.fact: He fought in the Spanish Civil War and was shot through the throat by a sniper, surviving to write two of the most influential political novels of the 20th century.

person.name: Franz Kafka
person.fact: He asked his friend Max Brod to burn all his manuscripts after his death; Brod ignored the request, giving the world The Trial and The Metamorphosis.

person.name: Virginia Woolf
person.fact: She helped pioneer the stream-of-consciousness technique in novels like Mrs Dalloway, and co-founded the Hogarth Press from her living room.

person.name: Oscar Wilde
person.fact: He was sentenced to two years of hard labor for gross indecency and wrote his most heartbreaking work, The Ballad of Reading Gaol, from prison.

person.name: Arthur Conan Doyle
person.fact: He tried to kill off Sherlock Holmes at Reichenbach Falls in 1893, but public outcry was so fierce he was forced to bring the detective back from the dead.

person.name: Agatha Christie
person.fact: The best-selling mystery writer of all time; her whodunit play The Mousetrap has run continuously in London's West End since 1952

person.name: J.R.R. Tolkien
person.fact: He invented entire languages, including Quenya and Sindarin, before writing the stories of Middle-earth to give those languages a world to live in.

person.name: Victor Hugo
person.fact: He was exiled from France for opposing Napoleon III and wrote Les Misérables from the island of Guernsey, becoming a hero to the French working class.

person.name: Alexandre Dumas
person.fact: He wrote The Count of Monte Cristo and The Three Musketeers so prolifically that he was rumored to run a fiction factory with ghostwriters, which he enthusiastically denied.

person.name: Rembrandt
person.fact: He painted over 80 self-portraits across his lifetime, more than almost any artist in history, creating an unparalleled visual autobiography of aging and hardship.

person.name: Salvador Dalí
person.fact: He painted The Persistence of Memory, the melting clocks, in under two hours, reportedly inspired by staring at a piece of melting Camembert cheese.

person.name: Plato
person.fact: He was originally a wrestler who competed at the Isthmian Games, and his philosophical dialogues, featuring his teacher Socrates, still anchor university philosophy courses worldwide.

person.name: Aristotle
person.fact: He tutored a teenage Alexander the Great for three years and wrote foundational texts on logic, biology, ethics, and drama that shaped Western thought for two millennia.

person.name: Voltaire
person.fact: He was imprisoned in the Bastille twice and exiled from France, yet kept writing sharp satirical attacks on the Church and absolute monarchy from across the border.

person.name: Friedrich Nietzsche
person.fact: He declared God is dead not as a boast but as a warning, and spent his final decade in a state of mental collapse, cared for by his mother.

person.name: Immanuel Kant
person.fact: Proposed that moral actions must pass a single test: could you will everyone to act the same way? He called this the categorical imperative

person.name: René Descartes
person.fact: He wrote 'I think, therefore I am' while literally hiding in a warm stove room during winter, which he said was where his best ideas came from.

person.name: LeBron James
person.fact: He went straight from high school to the NBA and was on the cover of Sports Illustrated at 17, then delivered Cleveland its first major sports championship in 52 years in 2016.

person.name: Tiger Woods
person.fact: He won the 1997 Masters by 12 strokes, the largest margin in tournament history, at age 21, and went on to hold the world number one ranking for a record 683 weeks.

person.name: Roger Federer
person.fact: He won 20 Grand Slam singles titles and was known for a playing style so fluid that opponents and fans often described watching him as closer to art than sport.

person.name: Cristiano Ronaldo
person.fact: He is the all-time top scorer in international men's football and the first player to score at five different FIFA World Cups.

person.name: Lionel Messi
person.fact: He was diagnosed with a growth hormone deficiency as a child; Barcelona paid for his treatment at age 13 on the condition he moved from Argentina to join their academy.

person.name: Diego Maradona
person.fact: In a single 1986 World Cup quarter-final against England, he scored both the infamous Hand of God goal and what FIFA voted the Goal of the Century: in the same match.

person.name: Ayrton Senna
person.fact: He qualified on pole position 65 times in Formula 1, a record that stood for 16 years, and famously described entering a trance-like state while lapping Monaco in the wet.

person.name: Michael Schumacher
person.fact: He won seven Formula 1 World Championships, five of them consecutively, and still holds the record for most wins in a single season.

person.name: Jackie Robinson
person.fact: When he took the field in 1947, he became the first Black player in MLB's modern era; his jersey number, #42, is the only number retired across every team in baseball

person.name: Wayne Gretzky
person.fact: He scored so many points in his NHL career that if you removed all his goals, his assists alone would still make him the all-time leading scorer in league history.

person.name: Mike Tyson
person.fact: He became the youngest heavyweight boxing world champion in history at age 20, and was known for a ferocious style that ended fights in the opening rounds.

person.name: Steffi Graf
person.fact: She is the only tennis player, male or female, to win the Golden Slam: all four Grand Slams plus the Olympic gold medal in the same calendar year (1988).

person.name: Martina Navratilova
person.fact: She won 9 Wimbledon singles titles and was the world number one for a total of 332 weeks, often credited with transforming women's tennis into a power game.

person.name: Jane Goodall
person.fact: She arrived in Tanzania in 1960 with no formal degree and discovered that chimpanzees make and use tools, overturning the scientific definition of what makes humans unique.

person.name: Neil deGrasse Tyson
person.fact: He successfully campaigned for Pluto's reclassification as a dwarf planet in 2006, earning him both scientific acclaim and years of angry letters from Pluto fans.

person.name: Bill Nye
person.fact: His Saturday morning TV show made him the science teacher for an entire generation of 1990s kids, and he later became a prominent public voice on climate change.

person.name: David Attenborough
person.fact: Has been narrating natural history documentaries for over seven decades: his career spans from black-and-white BBC broadcasts in the 1950s to 4K streaming today

person.name: Buzz Aldrin
person.fact: He was the second human to walk on the Moon, and while still on the lunar surface, he privately took Communion, a fact NASA kept quiet for years.

person.name: Yuri Gagarin
person.fact: On April 12, 1961, he orbited Earth in 108 minutes aboard Vostok 1, becoming the first human in space, and was reportedly so calm during launch he whistled a folk song.

person.name: Sally Ride
person.fact: She became the first American woman in space in 1983, and after her death it was revealed she had been in a same-sex relationship for 27 years.

person.name: Florence Nightingale
person.fact: She used statistical charts, including an early form of the pie chart, to convince the British government that more soldiers were dying from preventable infection than from wounds.

person.name: Wright Brothers
person.fact: Orville and Wilbur's first powered flight at Kitty Hawk in 1903 lasted just 12 seconds, but they were so secretive about their work that the press largely ignored it for years.

person.name: Alexander Graham Bell
person.fact: He invented the telephone but reportedly found it so intrusive he refused to have one in his own study, preferring uninterrupted work.

person.name: Henry Ford
person.fact: He didn't invent the automobile, but he introduced the moving assembly line and paid his workers $5 a day, double the going rate, so they could afford to buy the cars they built.

person.name: Pythagoras
person.fact: He led a secretive religious cult in ancient Greece that banned followers from eating beans and believed that numbers held mystical cosmic power.

person.name: Euclid
person.fact: His geometry textbook Elements was used in schools for over 2,000 years and is considered the most successful textbook ever written.

person.name: Erwin Schrödinger
person.fact: He devised the famous thought experiment of a cat that is simultaneously alive and dead to mock what he saw as the absurdity of Copenhagen quantum mechanics.

person.name: J. Robert Oppenheimer
person.fact: He directed the Manhattan Project; watching the first atomic test, he later recalled a line from the Bhagavad Gita: 'Now I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds.'

person.name: Enrico Fermi
person.fact: He built the world's first nuclear reactor in a squash court under a university stadium in 1942, and posed the paradox that asks why, given the vastness of the universe, we have never heard from alien civilisations

person.name: Marie Antoinette
person.fact: Queen of France who was guillotined at 37 during the Revolution, forever linked to the phrase 'let them eat cake', which historians say she almost certainly never said

person.name: Hippocrates
person.fact: He is credited with separating medicine from superstition and divine punishment, insisting illness had natural causes, and his name still echoes in the oath doctors take today.

person.name: Grace Hopper
person.fact: She invented the first compiler and popularized the term 'debugging' after literally finding a moth in a computer relay; the world's largest gathering of women in tech is named after her

person.name: Wernher von Braun
person.fact: He designed the V-2 rocket for Nazi Germany, the world's first ballistic missile, then surrendered to the Americans and led the team that built the Saturn V rocket that took humans to the Moon.

person.name: Steve Jobs
person.fact: He was famously ousted from Apple in 1985, spent a decade building Pixar and NeXT, then returned to rescue a nearly bankrupt Apple and turned it into the most valuable company on Earth.

person.name: Bill Gates
person.fact: He dropped out of Harvard and co-founded Microsoft at 19, and his ruthless licensing deal for MS-DOS with IBM is often credited as the single move that made him a billionaire.

person.name: Elon Musk
person.fact: Paid $44 billion for Twitter, renamed it X, and triggered a mass advertiser exodus; also tweeted 'funding secured' about a Tesla buyout that never happened, leading to an SEC investigation

person.name: Mark Zuckerberg
person.fact: He launched Facebook from his Harvard dorm room in 2004 and by his late 20s had built a platform used by more than a billion people, and faced congressional testimony over its impact on democracy.

person.name: Jeff Bezos
person.fact: He started Amazon by packing books in his garage and later built a company so dominant it reshaped global retail, logistics, and cloud computing, and funded his own trip to space.

person.name: Larry Page
person.fact: He and Sergey Brin developed the PageRank algorithm as a Stanford PhD project, rating web pages by how many other pages link to them, turning it into the foundation of Google.

person.name: Sergey Brin
person.fact: He co-founded Google with Larry Page in a Stanford dorm, and the name came from a misspelling of 'googol': the number 1 followed by 100 zeros.

person.name: Steve Wozniak
person.fact: He single-handedly designed the Apple I and Apple II computers from scratch, and was so generous he sold his HP calculator to fund the first production run of the Apple I.

person.name: Sundar Pichai
person.fact: He rose from a middle-class family in Chennai, where his household had no car and he slept in the living room, to become CEO of Google and its parent company Alphabet.

person.name: Tim Cook
person.fact: He took over Apple after Steve Jobs's death in 2011 and made it the first public company to reach a $1 trillion market valuation, steering it through the smartphone era's peak.

person.name: Sam Altman
person.fact: He led OpenAI through the release of ChatGPT, the fastest-growing consumer application in history, and was dramatically fired and rehired by the board within five days in 2023.

person.name: Jensen Huang
person.fact: He co-founded Nvidia in 1993 and bet the company on graphics chips for gaming; decades later, those same chips became the essential hardware for training artificial intelligence.

person.name: Warren Buffett
person.fact: He bought his first stock at age 11 and has donated the vast majority of his multi-hundred-billion-dollar fortune to charity: mostly to the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation.

person.name: Oprah Winfrey
person.fact: She grew up in poverty in rural Mississippi and became the first Black female billionaire in history, with her book club recommendations alone capable of making novels instant bestsellers.

person.name: Martha Stewart
person.fact: She built a lifestyle empire worth hundreds of millions of dollars and served five months in federal prison for insider trading, then returned to television and rebuilt her brand.

person.name: Richard Branson
person.fact: He launched Virgin Records, Virgin Atlantic, and Virgin Galactic, and set multiple world records in hot-air balloon and powerboat journeys across the Atlantic and Pacific.

person.name: Donald Trump
person.fact: He was a New York real estate developer and reality TV host who became the 45th President, then the first former US president to be convicted of felony crimes.

person.name: Howard Hughes
person.fact: He was a record-breaking aviator, Hollywood producer, and one of the wealthiest people alive, before retreating into extreme reclusion and severe OCD in his later decades.

person.name: John D. Rockefeller
person.fact: He built Standard Oil into a monopoly controlling 90% of US oil refining, was forced to break it up by the Supreme Court in 1911, and the successor companies still rank among the world's largest.

person.name: Andrew Carnegie
person.fact: He sold his steel empire for nearly $500 million in 1901 and then gave almost all of it away, funding over 2,500 public libraries across the English-speaking world.

person.name: J.P. Morgan
person.fact: He personally organized a private bailout of the US government during the 1893 financial crisis and again stopped a banking collapse in 1907: effectively acting as a one-man central bank.

person.name: Estée Lauder
person.fact: She built her cosmetics empire from scratch by insisting on giving away free samples, a then-radical idea, and turned a kitchen recipe into one of the world's most valuable beauty companies.

person.name: Barack Obama
person.fact: He became the first African American President of the United States in 2009 and was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in the first year of his presidency.

person.name: Michelle Obama
person.fact: Her Let's Move! campaign to combat childhood obesity and her memoir Becoming, which sold over 17 million copies, made her one of the most admired public figures in the world.

person.name: Joe Biden
person.fact: He was first elected to the US Senate at 29, too young to be seated, and decades later became the oldest person ever inaugurated as President at age 78.

person.name: Kamala Harris
person.fact: She became the first woman, first Black American, and first person of South Asian descent to serve as Vice President of the United States.

person.name: Hillary Clinton
person.fact: She was the first woman nominated for president by a major US political party, served as Secretary of State, and won the 2016 popular vote while losing the Electoral College.

person.name: Bill Clinton
person.fact: He survived an impeachment trial over the Monica Lewinsky affair and left office with the highest end-of-term approval rating of any US president since World War II.

person.name: George W. Bush
person.fact: He launched the invasion of Afghanistan and Iraq following the September 11 attacks, reshaping US foreign policy and the Middle East for decades.

person.name: George H. W. Bush
person.fact: He led a 35-nation coalition to expel Iraq from Kuwait in the Gulf War but famously broke his 'read my lips: no new taxes' pledge, costing him a second term.

person.name: Ronald Reagan
person.fact: A former Hollywood actor, he became the 40th President and is credited, controversially, with accelerating the end of the Cold War through military spending and direct diplomacy with Gorbachev.

person.name: Jimmy Carter
person.fact: A peanut farmer from Georgia who became President, and after losing re-election went on to become arguably the most productive ex-president in US history, building houses with Habitat for Humanity into his 90s

person.name: Richard Nixon
person.fact: The only US President to resign from office, stepping down in 1974 over the Watergate break-in and cover-up; he had also opened diplomatic relations with China

person.name: Lyndon B. Johnson
person.fact: He signed the Civil Rights Act and Voting Rights Act into law, transforming American society; his escalation of the Vietnam War consumed his presidency and led him to decline re-election

person.name: Dwight D. Eisenhower
person.fact: He commanded the Allied forces on D-Day during World War II, then as President launched the Interstate Highway System and warned of the growing 'military-industrial complex.'

person.name: Harry S. Truman
person.fact: He made the decision to drop atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki to end World War II, a call that remains one of the most debated in modern history.

person.name: Andrew Jackson
person.fact: He survived two assassination attempts and was famous for his violent temper: he dueled over 100 times and carried a bullet in his chest for the rest of his life.

person.name: Ulysses S. Grant
person.fact: He accepted Robert E. Lee's surrender to end the Civil War and, facing bankruptcy from a financial fraud near the end of his life, finished his acclaimed memoirs just days before his death.

person.name: Benjamin Franklin
person.fact: He flew a kite in a thunderstorm to prove lightning was electricity, invented bifocals and the lightning rod, and negotiated the alliance with France that won the American Revolution.

person.name: Alexander Hamilton
person.fact: He was orphaned as a teenager in the Caribbean and talked his way to America, became George Washington's right-hand man, and founded the US financial system: before dying in a duel with the sitting Vice President.

person.name: Bernie Sanders
person.fact: Ran two strong campaigns for the Democratic presidential nomination, winning millions of votes on a platform of wealth inequality and universal healthcare he had championed in Congress for decades

person.name: Nancy Pelosi
person.fact: She became the first woman elected Speaker of the House and famously tore up a copy of Donald Trump's State of the Union address on national television behind his back.

person.name: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez
person.fact: She was working as a bartender in New York when she ran for Congress in 2018 and, at 29, became the youngest woman ever elected to the US House of Representatives.

person.name: Mitch McConnell
person.fact: He blocked President Obama's Supreme Court nominee Merrick Garland from receiving a hearing for nearly a year, then filled three Supreme Court seats during the Trump administration.

person.name: John McCain
person.fact: He was a prisoner of war in Hanoi for over five years, refused early release to avoid giving propaganda value to his captors, and decades later became a Senate maverick known for bipartisan deals.

person.name: Mitt Romney
person.fact: He made a fortune in private equity, rescued the 2002 Salt Lake City Olympics from a corruption scandal, and later became the first sitting senator to vote to convict a president of his own party.

person.name: Al Gore
person.fact: He won the popular vote in the 2000 presidential election but lost the presidency when the Supreme Court halted a Florida recount, then won the Nobel Peace Prize for his climate advocacy.

person.name: Dick Cheney
person.fact: He was widely considered the most powerful Vice President in US history, a key architect of the Iraq War, and famously shot a friend in the face on a quail hunting trip.

person.name: Ruth Bader Ginsburg
person.fact: She was one of only nine women in her Harvard Law class in 1956 and spent her career dismantling gender discrimination laws, becoming a pop-culture icon known simply as 'the Notorious RBG.'

person.name: Antonin Scalia
person.fact: He championed originalism, interpreting the Constitution strictly as written, and formed an unlikely but genuine friendship with his ideological opposite, Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg.

person.name: Sonia Sotomayor
person.fact: She grew up in a Bronx housing project and was diagnosed with diabetes at age 8, going on to become the first Latina Supreme Court Justice in US history.

person.name: Sandra Day O'Connor
person.fact: She became the first woman on the US Supreme Court in 1981 after Ronald Reagan appointed her, often serving as the decisive swing vote on contentious cases for two decades.

person.name: Justin Trudeau
person.fact: He is the son of a former Prime Minister and became Canada's second-youngest PM, initially riding a wave of progressive optimism before facing a series of political scandals.

person.name: Pierre Trudeau
person.fact: He was a flamboyant intellectual who pirouetted behind the Queen's back, dated Barbra Streisand, and invoked the War Measures Act against Québec separatist terrorists in 1970.

person.name: Boris Johnson
person.fact: He was the face of the successful Brexit campaign, became Prime Minister, and then resigned in disgrace after a series of scandals including parties held at Downing Street during COVID lockdowns.

person.name: Tony Blair
person.fact: He won a landslide election in 1997 ending 18 years of Conservative rule, brokered the Good Friday Agreement in Northern Ireland, and then saw his legacy overshadowed by the invasion of Iraq.

person.name: David Cameron
person.fact: He called the 2016 Brexit referendum expecting to win it and settle the issue, then resigned within hours of losing, leaving Britain in a political crisis that lasted nearly a decade.

person.name: Rishi Sunak
person.fact: He became Britain's first Prime Minister of Indian heritage and its youngest in over 200 years, then led the Conservative Party to its worst election defeat in nearly a century.

person.name: Keir Starmer
person.fact: He was a human rights lawyer who led the Crown Prosecution Service before entering politics, then won a landslide victory to become Prime Minister in 2024, ending 14 years of Conservative government.

person.name: Angela Merkel
person.fact: She was a quantum chemist from East Germany who became Chancellor for 16 years, making her the longest-serving leader of any major Western democracy of her era.

person.name: Emmanuel Macron
person.fact: He founded his own political movement from scratch and became France's youngest-ever President at 39, having previously been advised in part by his wife: his former high school drama teacher.

person.name: Vladimir Putin
person.fact: A former KGB intelligence officer, he has dominated Russian politics for over two decades and ordered the full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, triggering Europe's largest land war since World War II.

person.name: Volodymyr Zelenskyy
person.fact: A former comedian and TV star, he refused evacuation after Russia's 2022 invasion with the now-iconic line: 'I need ammunition, not a ride.'

person.name: Xi Jinping
person.fact: Oversaw the largest anti-corruption campaign in the Communist Party's history, investigating over a million officials, and presided over the period that lifted more people out of extreme poverty than any government in history

person.name: Kim Jong Un
person.fact: He ordered the execution of his own uncle Jang Song-thaek in 2013 and later met with Donald Trump in three historic, and ultimately fruitless, summits.

person.name: Kim Jong Il
person.fact: He reportedly had a personal collection of over 20,000 films and kidnapped a South Korean filmmaker to build a North Korean movie industry.

person.name: Benjamin Netanyahu
person.fact: The longest-serving Prime Minister in Israel's history, he was re-elected while under indictment on corruption charges and oversaw the military campaign in Gaza that international courts have described as genocide

person.name: Narendra Modi
person.fact: He rose from selling tea as a child to leading India's 1.4 billion people, and his 2014 landslide win ended a decade of Congress rule by one of the largest democratic mandates in history.

person.name: Shinzo Abe
person.fact: Japan's longest-serving prime minister, he was assassinated while giving a campaign speech in 2022: the first such killing of a major Japanese leader in modern times.

person.name: Mikhail Gorbachev
person.fact: His policies of glasnost and perestroika inadvertently unravelled the Soviet Union, making him the last leader of a superpower that ceased to exist on his watch.

person.name: Boris Yeltsin
person.fact: He stood on a tank outside the Russian parliament in 1991 to defy a coup attempt, then on New Year's Eve 1999 unexpectedly resigned live on television, handing power to a little-known Vladimir Putin.

person.name: Hugo Chávez
person.fact: He survived a coup attempt in 2002, then used Venezuela's oil wealth to fund a pan-Latin American socialist movement he called the Bolivarian Revolution.

person.name: Saddam Hussein
person.fact: He used chemical weapons on his own Kurdish citizens at Halabja in 1988, killing thousands, and was later found hiding in an underground hole before his execution in 2006.

person.name: Muammar Gaddafi
person.fact: He ruled Libya for 42 years with a blend of pan-African ideology and eccentric theatrics, including travelling with an all-female bodyguard unit and pitching a tent in foreign capitals.

person.name: Yasser Arafat
person.fact: He shared the Nobel Peace Prize in 1994 after signing the Oslo Accords, yet spent his final years under Israeli military siege in his Ramallah compound.

person.name: Anwar Sadat
person.fact: He flew to Jerusalem in 1977, a stunning act no Arab leader had attempted, and became the first Arab head of state to officially recognise Israel, earning a Nobel Peace Prize and later an assassin's bullet.

person.name: Leila Khaled
person.fact: The first woman to hijack an airplane, she became an iconic image of the Palestinian resistance movement: her keffiyeh-wrapped portrait one of the most reproduced political photographs of the 20th century

person.name: Indira Gandhi
person.fact: She declared a state of emergency in 1975, suspending democracy for nearly two years, then was assassinated in 1984 by her own Sikh bodyguards after ordering troops to storm the Golden Temple.

person.name: Pope Francis
person.fact: The first Jesuit and first Latin American pope, he chose a name no pope had ever used, a deliberate nod to a life of simplicity, and famously asked 'Who am I to judge?' about gay priests

person.name: Pope John Paul II
person.fact: He survived an assassination attempt in St. Peter's Square in 1981, later personally visiting and forgiving his attacker in prison, and his 1979 visit to Poland helped ignite the Solidarity movement that cracked communist rule.

person.name: Pope Benedict XVI
person.fact: The first pope to resign in nearly 600 years, he stepped down in 2013 citing failing strength, stunning a Church that had assumed the papacy was a lifetime office.

person.name: Mother Teresa
person.fact: She founded the Missionaries of Charity in Calcutta's slums in 1950 and won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1979, refusing the traditional banquet and asking that its cost be donated to the poor instead.

person.name: Dalai Lama
person.fact: Identified as the reincarnation of his predecessor at age two, he has led the Tibetan government-in-exile from Dharamsala, India since fleeing a Chinese crackdown in 1959.

person.name: Adolf Hitler
person.fact: A failed Austrian artist who rose to Chancellor of Germany in 1933, he orchestrated the systematic murder of six million Jews and plunged the world into a war that killed over 70 million people.

person.name: Benito Mussolini
person.fact: The inventor of modern fascism, he made the trains run on time, or so the myth went, and was killed by partisans in 1945, his body hung upside down at a Milan petrol station.

person.name: Osama bin Laden
person.fact: The Saudi-born architect of the September 11, 2001 attacks, he evaded capture for nearly a decade before U.S. Navy SEALs killed him at a compound in Abbottabad, Pakistan in 2011.

person.name: Henry Kissinger
person.fact: Accused of orchestrating war crimes including the secret bombing of Cambodia, yet won the Nobel Peace Prize so controversially that two committee members resigned in protest

person.name: Colin Powell
person.fact: A four-star general who became the first Black U.S. Secretary of State, he later called his 2003 UN speech presenting the case for WMDs in Iraq a 'blot' that would forever stain his record.

person.name: Condoleezza Rice
person.fact: The first Black woman to serve as U.S. Secretary of State, she was National Security Advisor on 9/11 and a concert-level classical pianist who once performed alongside cellist Yo-Yo Ma.

person.name: Madeleine Albright
person.fact: The first female U.S. Secretary of State, she famously communicated diplomatic messages through her brooch choices, a habit she called 'pin diplomacy', and had fled Czechoslovakia as a child refugee.

person.name: King Charles III
person.fact: He waited longer than any heir in British history to ascend the throne, becoming king at 73, and had championed environmental causes and organic farming for decades before it was fashionable.

person.name: Princess Diana
person.fact: Her 1997 funeral drew two billion television viewers worldwide, and her landmine-clearing campaign in Angola, broadcast globally just months before her death, helped accelerate an international ban on the weapons.

person.name: Prince William
person.fact: He served as an RAF search-and-rescue helicopter pilot before royal duties took over, and his surprise 2010 proposal to Kate Middleton used his late mother Diana's sapphire engagement ring.

person.name: Prince Harry
person.fact: He served two combat tours in Afghanistan, making him the first British royal to see active front-line duty in 25 years, before later resigning as a senior royal and moving to California.

person.name: Meghan Markle
person.fact: A mixed-race American actress best known for 'Suits,' she became the first divorced, biracial American to marry into the modern British royal family, and the couple's 2021 Oprah interview sent shockwaves through the monarchy.

person.name: Kate Middleton
person.fact: A commoner who met Prince William at St. Andrews University, she became the first future queen consort to earn a university degree, and her fashion choices routinely sell out within minutes.

person.name: Prince Philip
person.fact: He served in the Royal Navy during WWII and was present at Japan's formal surrender in Tokyo Bay, then spent 73 years as Queen Elizabeth II's consort: the longest-serving royal consort in British history.

person.name: Eleanor Roosevelt
person.fact: She transformed the ceremonial role of First Lady into an activist platform, writing a daily newspaper column and becoming the first U.S. delegate to the UN, where she helped draft the Universal Declaration of Human Rights.

person.name: Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis
person.fact: She was seated beside her husband when he was shot dead in a Dallas motorcade in 1963; she later remarried a Greek shipping billionaire and quietly built a second career as a book editor in New York.

person.name: Malcolm X
person.fact: He rejected integration and demanded Black self-determination at a time when that was considered radical, building a mass movement around pride, economic independence, and armed self-defence; a pilgrimage to Mecca transformed his views in the final year of his life.

person.name: Rosa Parks
person.fact: Her refusal to give up her bus seat in Montgomery, Alabama in 1955 was not a spontaneous act of tiredness: she was a trained NAACP activist whose arrest had been carefully chosen to spark the boycott.

person.name: Frederick Douglass
person.fact: An escaped slave who taught himself to read in secret, he became the most photographed American of the 19th century and wrote one of the most searing autobiographies in the English language.

person.name: Susan B. Anthony
person.fact: She was arrested and fined $100 for voting in the 1872 presidential election, and died 14 years before the amendment bearing her name gave women the vote.

person.name: Malala Yousafzai
person.fact: Shot in the head by a Taliban gunman on her school bus in Pakistan's Swat Valley at age 15, she survived to become the youngest-ever Nobel Peace Prize laureate and continues to campaign for girls' education.

person.name: Greta Thunberg
person.fact: She started her climate protest alone outside the Swedish parliament at age 15 with a hand-painted sign, and within two years had inspired millions of students worldwide to strike from school every Friday.

person.name: Anne Frank
person.fact: She kept a diary for two years while hiding with her family in a concealed Amsterdam apartment during the Nazi occupation; her father Otto was the only family member to survive the camps and published her words.

person.name: Tom Hanks
person.fact: The only actor to win back-to-back Best Actor Oscars since Spencer Tracy, he achieved it for 'Philadelphia' and 'Forrest Gump' in 1994 and 1995.

person.name: Tom Cruise
person.fact: He performed his own stunts, including clinging to the outside of a moving Airbus A400M at takeoff, and has been synonymous with the Mission: Impossible franchise for three decades.

person.name: Brad Pitt
person.fact: He spent years eating on screen in nearly every film as a deliberate character choice, and his production company Plan B brought 'The Departed,' '12 Years a Slave,' and 'Moonlight' to the Oscars.

person.name: Angelina Jolie
person.fact: She became a UN Goodwill Ambassador after filming 'Lara Croft' in Cambodia, going on to make over 60 field visits to refugee camps worldwide and adopt three children from Cambodia, Ethiopia, and Vietnam.

person.name: Leonardo DiCaprio
person.fact: He was nominated for an Oscar five times before finally winning for 'The Revenant' in 2016, during which he actually ate raw bison liver on camera despite being a vegetarian.

person.name: Robert De Niro
person.fact: He gained 60 pounds to play the older Jake LaMotta in 'Raging Bull' and actually trained as a boxer so convincingly that LaMotta said he could have gone professional.

person.name: Al Pacino
person.fact: His 'hoo-ah!' in 'Scent of a Woman' earned him his only Oscar after seven previous nominations, and he reportedly based his Scarface accent on a Cuban friend rather than research.

person.name: Jack Nicholson
person.fact: A courtside fixture at LA Lakers games for decades, he discovered at age 37 that the woman he'd grown up believing was his sister was actually his mother.

person.name: Robert Redford
person.fact: He co-founded the Sundance Film Festival in Utah, turning it into the world's premier showcase for independent cinema and single-handedly reshaping how non-studio films reach audiences.

person.name: Paul Newman
person.fact: He donated all profits from his Newman's Own food brand to charity, ultimately over $600 million, and was still competitive racing cars into his late seventies.

person.name: Clint Eastwood
person.fact: He served as mayor of Carmel-by-the-Sea, California in the 1980s, and directed 'Million Dollar Baby' and 'Unforgiven', both Best Picture Oscar winners, decades into his career.

person.name: Harrison Ford
person.fact: He was working as a carpenter installing a door at a studio when George Lucas spotted him and cast him as Han Solo, launching one of Hollywood's most durable careers.

person.name: Sean Connery
person.fact: He was voted 'Sexiest Man of the Century' by People magazine at age 69, and reportedly learned his iconic rolled 'r' from a speech therapist while preparing to play James Bond.

person.name: Anthony Hopkins
person.fact: He won the Oscar for Best Actor for 'The Silence of the Lambs' with only about 16 minutes of screen time, one of the shortest performances ever to claim the award, and is also a published composer.

person.name: Morgan Freeman
person.fact: He didn't land his first major film role until his early fifties, and his narration is so distinctive that NASA used his voice to introduce the Deep Impact mission to the public.

person.name: Samuel L. Jackson
person.fact: A recovering addict who got sober at 40, he has since become the highest-grossing actor in box-office history and reportedly carries a favourite golf club on every film set.

person.name: Denzel Washington
person.fact: The first Black actor to win two Academy Awards, he turned down the role of Morpheus in 'The Matrix' and has said his greatest performance is his stage work: particularly 'Fences,' which he also directed.

person.name: Will Smith
person.fact: He walked on stage at the Oscars in 2022 and slapped presenter Chris Rock, then minutes later won Best Actor for 'King Richard' in one of the most shocking moments in the ceremony's history.

person.name: Eddie Murphy
person.fact: His 1983 stand-up special 'Delirious' is considered one of the greatest in history, and at his peak in the mid-1980s he was the highest-paid entertainer in the world.

person.name: Robin Williams
person.fact: An improvisational genius who ad-libbed so relentlessly that the script for 'Aladdin' was rewritten around his riffs, he was posthumously diagnosed with Lewy body dementia, a disease his widow called 'the terrorist in his brain.'

person.name: Jim Carrey
person.fact: He wrote himself a cheque for $10 million for 'acting services rendered' when he was broke and struggling, dated it for Thanksgiving 1995, and 'Dumb and Dumber' came out just before that date and made him exactly that.

person.name: Adam Sandler
person.fact: Notoriously passed over by mainstream awards, he threatened to make the worst movie ever if he didn't get an Oscar nomination for 'Uncut Gems', and his performance is still cited as one of the finest of that year.

person.name: Bill Murray
person.fact: He is famously uncontactable, having no agent or manager, only a phone number that leads to a voicemail, which means directors must persist for months, lending his cameos an air of legend.

person.name: Steve Martin
person.fact: He pioneered the 'anti-comedian' in the 1970s, filling arenas with absurdist non-jokes, then reinvented himself as a respected playwright, novelist, and bluegrass banjo player.

person.name: Mike Myers
person.fact: He created the Austin Powers franchise on a $16 million budget that grossed over $312 million globally, and based Dr. Evil partly on his old Saturday Night Live producer Lorne Michaels.

person.name: Ben Stiller
person.fact: He wrote and directed 'Zoolander' as a short film for the 1996 VH1 Fashion Awards before it became a feature, and the blue steel look was lifted from an actual modelling pose he spotted in a magazine.

person.name: Dustin Hoffman
person.fact: Famously method to a fault: he stayed awake for days to play an exhausted character in 'Marathon Man,' prompting co-star Laurence Olivier's exasperated response: 'Have you tried acting, dear boy?'

person.name: Sylvester Stallone
person.fact: He wrote the script for 'Rocky' in three days and refused to sell it unless he could star in it, turning down offers of up to $350,000 to cast a bigger name, and the film won Best Picture.

person.name: Arnold Schwarzenegger
person.fact: He won the Mr. Olympia bodybuilding title seven times, then became a Hollywood action star, then served two terms as Governor of California, a trajectory no one else in history has matched.

person.name: Bruce Willis
person.fact: He was a struggling bartender in New York when he landed 'Moonlighting' over tens of thousands of auditionees, and 'Die Hard', initially considered a risky casting choice, redefined the action genre.

person.name: Mel Gibson
person.fact: He was broke and sleeping in a friend's car when he auditioned for 'Mad Max' in 1979 after being beaten up the night before: the bruises apparently convincing the director he was perfect for the role.

person.name: Kevin Costner
person.fact: He financed the $175 million epic 'Waterworld' himself when studios balked at the budget, and despite being a critical punching bag, the film eventually turned a profit on home video.

person.name: Michael Douglas
person.fact: He produced 'One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest', which swept all five major Oscars, before he was a household name as an actor, having inherited the rights from his father Kirk.

person.name: Kirk Douglas
person.fact: He was one of the few Hollywood stars to publicly break the McCarthy-era blacklist by crediting screenwriter Dalton Trumbo, a communist list member, on 'Spartacus' in 1960.

person.name: Gene Hackman
person.fact: His detective in 'The French Connection' improvised the now-classic line 'Have you ever picked your feet in Poughkeepsie?' mid-interrogation, and the suspect's confused reaction was real.

person.name: Christopher Walken
person.fact: He re-punctuates every script he receives by removing all the commas, creating his own rhythm, a technique he says makes dialogue feel more spontaneous, and was once a lion tamer.

person.name: Johnny Depp
person.fact: He based his Jack Sparrow performance on Keith Richards of the Rolling Stones and a rubber-legged cartoon character, delivering the walk to Disney executives who feared they'd hired a drunk.

person.name: Keanu Reeves
person.fact: He gave away roughly $80 million of his 'Matrix' earnings to the costume and special effects crews, quietly and without fanfare, and rides the New York subway like any other commuter.

person.name: Matt Damon
person.fact: He co-wrote 'Good Will Hunting' with Ben Affleck as a Harvard class assignment, was repeatedly told it was too good to be written by unknowns, and both were paid $600,000 for the script.

person.name: Ben Affleck
person.fact: After a career slump so severe it spawned the term 'Bennifer fatigue,' he reinvented himself as a director with 'Gone Baby Gone' and 'The Town' before winning Best Picture for 'Argo.'

person.name: George Clooney
person.fact: He was rejected from the acting programme at Northern Kentucky University and spent years in failed TV pilots before ER made him a star, a story he regularly cites as proof that persistence beats early talent.

person.name: Hugh Jackman
person.fact: He was cast as Wolverine two weeks into filming 'X-Men' after Dougray Scott dropped out, then played the same character for 17 years across nine films, a record for a superhero role.

person.name: Russell Crowe
person.fact: He beat out Tom Hanks, Tom Cruise, and Mel Gibson for 'Gladiator' and won the Oscar, despite the film having no finished script on the first day of shooting in Morocco.

person.name: Christian Bale
person.fact: He lost 63 pounds, down to 121 lbs, for 'The Machinist,' then gained it all back plus muscle in six months for 'Batman Begins,' setting a benchmark for physical transformation that few actors have matched.

person.name: Daniel Day-Lewis
person.fact: The only three-time Best Actor Oscar winner in history, he spent 18 months learning to use a left foot for 'My Left Foot' and famously refused to break character throughout filming, including being pushed to set in a wheelchair.

person.name: Daniel Craig
person.fact: He arrived as the most controversial Bond casting in franchise history, with a petition to sack him before filming began, then delivered what many critics consider the best Bond performance in 'Casino Royale.'

person.name: Pierce Brosnan
person.fact: He was cast as James Bond in 1986 but contractually blocked by the show 'Remington Steele,' which was renewed at the last minute, then had to wait nine more years before finally getting the role.

person.name: Hugh Grant
person.fact: His career nearly ended after a 1995 arrest for lewd conduct in Los Angeles, but his self-deprecating 'I did a very bad thing' interview with Jay Leno is credited with saving it.

person.name: Colin Firth
person.fact: His wet-shirt emergence from a lake as Mr. Darcy in the 1995 BBC 'Pride and Prejudice' is ranked one of the greatest moments in British television history, and the image was later commemorated with a giant lake statue.

person.name: Liam Neeson
person.fact: He trained as an amateur boxer in Belfast before becoming an actor, and his 'particular set of skills' speech in 'Taken', written in an afternoon, became one of the most quoted monologues of the 2000s.

person.name: Ian McKellen
person.fact: He came out as gay in 1988 during a BBC radio debate on Section 28, an act of courage at the time, and has since said he refuses to act in any production for a country that criminalises homosexuality.

person.name: Patrick Stewart
person.fact: He was so unknown when cast as Captain Picard that the 'Star Trek: TNG' crew assumed the bald Englishman was a joke, yet he went on to redefine the franchise and earn a knighthood.

person.name: Helen Mirren
person.fact: She won the Oscar, Emmy, Tony, and BAFTA, all four major English-language acting awards, and was made a Dame for services to drama, yet is perhaps best known for her bikini photos at 63.

person.name: Judi Dench
person.fact: She won her Oscar for 'Shakespeare in Love' with just eight minutes of screen time as Queen Elizabeth I, one of the shortest winning performances in history, and has macular degeneration that leaves her nearly blind.

person.name: Maggie Smith
person.fact: She was filming 'Downton Abbey' during chemotherapy for breast cancer, and many of the Dowager Countess's devastating one-liners were delivered between treatments.

person.name: Meryl Streep
person.fact: With 21 Academy Award nominations, far more than any other actor, she is known for total dialect immersion; for 'Sophie's Choice' she learned both Polish and German, and co-stars reported she would answer only in character.

person.name: Julia Roberts
person.fact: Her fee for 'Erin Brockovich', $20 million, made her the first actress to earn that sum for a single film, and she spent three months working at the real Brockovich's law firm in preparation.

person.name: Sandra Bullock
person.fact: She reportedly paid $1 million out of her own pocket to insure the troubled production of 'Speed,' and later won the Oscar for 'The Blind Side' while receiving a Razzie nomination in the same week.

person.name: Nicole Kidman
person.fact: After her divorce from Tom Cruise, she reportedly told friends 'at least I can wear heels now,' and went on to win the Oscar for 'The Hours' wearing a prosthetic nose.

person.name: Cate Blanchett
person.fact: She is one of very few actors to win Oscars in both leading and supporting categories, and co-directed Sydney Theatre Company while simultaneously shooting Hollywood blockbusters.

person.name: Charlize Theron
person.fact: A former ballet dancer who tore a knee ligament at 16, she pivoted to acting after being spotted arguing with a bank teller in a Los Angeles lobby: the manager called her a talent agent.

person.name: Halle Berry
person.fact: Her Best Actress win for 'Monster's Ball' in 2002 made her the first, and as of 2026, still the only, Black woman to win the Academy Award for Best Actress.

person.name: Whoopi Goldberg
person.fact: One of the few EGOT winners, Emmy, Grammy, Oscar, Tony, she began her career performing stand-up in a fish-and-chips restaurant and later joined 'The View' as a way to keep working during a Hollywood dry spell.

person.name: Reese Witherspoon
person.fact: She gained 15 pounds to play June Carter Cash in 'Walk the Line' and did all her own singing; Cash's family said she captured June better than June captured herself.

person.name: Jennifer Aniston
person.fact: She was told by an NBC executive early in her career that she was 'too fat' for television; 'Friends' debuted the following year and became one of the most-watched shows in history.

person.name: Jennifer Lawrence
person.fact: She became the second-youngest Best Actress Oscar winner in history for 'Silver Linings Playbook,' beating out fellow nominees Meryl Streep and Jessica Chastain at age 22.

person.name: Anne Hathaway
person.fact: She was cast in 'The Princess Diaries' after a casting director noticed she was the only actress who didn't fall out of her chair during auditions: she later won the Oscar for 'Les Misérables.'

person.name: Scarlett Johansson
person.fact: The highest-grossing actress in box-office history, she played Black Widow for over a decade across the MCU and sued Disney in 2021 over 'Black Widow's' streaming release, a suit settled out of court.

person.name: Natalie Portman
person.fact: She enrolled at Harvard to study psychology after 'Star Wars' made her famous as a teenager, and her 2016 Harvard commencement speech on the relationship between fear and creativity went viral.

person.name: Drew Barrymore
person.fact: She appeared on the cover of People magazine at age seven as a cast member of 'E.T.,' had completed rehab by age thirteen, and later rebuilt her career into a multi-platform media and beauty empire.

person.name: Cameron Diaz
person.fact: She was a model with no acting experience when she auditioned for 'The Mask', she didn't even know how to audition, and her natural chemistry with Jim Carrey launched her career.

person.name: Gwyneth Paltrow
person.fact: She won an Oscar for Shakespeare in Love but became equally famous for founding Goop, a lifestyle brand that once sold a jade egg with dubious wellness claims.

person.name: Demi Moore
person.fact: She shaved her head and earned a then-record $12.5 million for Striptease, but her most iconic role involved a pottery wheel and the song 'Unchained Melody'.

person.name: Sharon Stone
person.fact: A single scene in Basic Instinct made her a global star overnight and launched endless debates about what exactly happened during that police interrogation.

person.name: Salma Hayek
person.fact: She spent years fighting Harvey Weinstein to get her passion project made, a biopic about Frida Kahlo, and earned an Oscar nomination for playing the artist herself.

person.name: Penélope Cruz
person.fact: She became the first Spanish actress to win an Oscar, taking home the award for Vicky Cristina Barcelona after a string of acclaimed roles in Pedro Almodóvar films.

person.name: Sophia Loren
person.fact: She was once jailed for tax evasion in Italy, but history remembers her as the first performer to win an Oscar for a non-English-language performance, in Two Women.

person.name: Brigitte Bardot
person.fact: She was so mobbed by paparazzi in Saint-Tropez that she effectively invented celebrity media culture, then walked away from stardom at 39 to campaign for animal rights.

person.name: Elizabeth Taylor
person.fact: She was the first actress paid $1 million for a single film, Cleopatra, and her violet eyes, eight marriages, and towering jewel collection became as legendary as her performances.

person.name: Bette Davis
person.fact: She sued her own studio for the right to choose her own roles, a legal battle she lost but which eventually transformed how Hollywood treated its contract players.

person.name: Katharine Hepburn
person.fact: She holds the all-time record for most Best Actress Oscar wins with four, and wore trousers so defiantly in an era that banned them on studio lots that security once confiscated her clothes.

person.name: Judy Garland
person.fact: She recorded 'Somewhere Over the Rainbow' at 16 and the song became one of the most beloved in history, though the studio tried to cut it from The Wizard of Oz as too slow.

person.name: Shirley Temple
person.fact: She was the number-one box office star in America for four straight years during the Great Depression, cheering up a nation while still in primary school.

person.name: Lucille Ball
person.fact: She was so determined to star alongside her real-life husband that she bought out the studio, making her the first woman to run a major Hollywood production company.

person.name: Mary Tyler Moore
person.fact: Her sitcom's theme song, 'You're Gonna Make It After All', became an anthem for working women in the 1970s, and the hat-toss that opened it became one of TV's most iconic images.

person.name: Carrie Fisher
person.fact: She co-wrote the screenplay for When Harry Met Sally and turned her own struggles with addiction into a celebrated memoir, but the world knows her best as the princess who carried her own rescue.

person.name: Mark Hamill
person.fact: After his iconic sci-fi role he reinvented himself as one of the most acclaimed voice actors in history, voicing the Joker in Batman: The Animated Series for decades.

person.name: Robert Downey Jr.
person.fact: He went from a career-threatening prison stint to becoming the highest-paid actor in Hollywood, anchoring a cinematic universe that grossed over $20 billion.

person.name: Chris Hemsworth
person.fact: He beat out thousands of actors, including his own brother, to win the role of a Norse god, then transformed his physique so dramatically that the costume team had to rebuild his suit.

person.name: Chris Evans
person.fact: He almost turned down the role of Captain America due to anxiety about the commitment, but the character's decade-long arc became one of the most beloved in superhero cinema.

person.name: Chris Pratt
person.fact: He went from playing a loveable schlub on a network sitcom to leading a cosmic superhero team, losing 60 pounds in six months for the audition that changed his career.

person.name: Ryan Reynolds
person.fact: After a superhero film so poorly received that it was used as a punchline, he bought back the rights to the character and produced his own version, turning it into a billion-dollar franchise.

person.name: Ryan Gosling
person.fact: He was cast in The Notebook specifically because the director thought he was 'not handsome' enough: the film became one of the defining romance movies of the 2000s.

person.name: Idris Elba
person.fact: He hid his British accent so thoroughly while playing a Baltimore drug kingpin that American audiences were stunned to learn he was from London when he appeared at awards shows.

person.name: Dwayne Johnson
person.fact: He was cut from the Canadian Football League with seven dollars in his pocket before pivoting to wrestling, where he became one of the most electrifying performers in the sport's history.

person.name: Vin Diesel
person.fact: He wrote, directed, and starred in a short film just to get himself noticed in Hollywood: it worked, landing him a role in Saving Private Ryan and launching one of cinema's longest-running franchises.

person.name: Jason Statham
person.fact: He was a competitive diver who represented England at the 1992 World Championships before a chance encounter with a fashion photographer set him on the path to action stardom.

person.name: Jackie Chan
person.fact: He performs virtually all his own stunts and has broken nearly every bone in his body doing so: his outtake reels of failed and painful stunt attempts became a beloved cinema tradition.

person.name: Bruce Lee
person.fact: He was so fast that film crews had to shoot him at 32 frames per second instead of 24 just to make his punches visible on screen.

person.name: Jet Li
person.fact: He won China's national wushu championship five consecutive times starting at age 11, and turned down a role as a bodyguard for Richard Nixon to focus on martial arts.

person.name: Timothée Chalamet
person.fact: At 22 he became one of the youngest Best Actor Oscar nominees in history for Call Me by Your Name, kicking off a run of prestige roles that made him the defining leading man of his generation.

person.name: Tom Holland
person.fact: He kept his casting as Spider-Man secret for months, revealing it accidentally on Instagram: the social-media slip became almost as famous as his first appearance in the suit.

person.name: Zendaya
person.fact: She became the youngest person ever to win the Best Drama Actress Emmy, then won it again two years later, for playing a teenager navigating addiction in Euphoria.

person.name: Margot Robbie
person.fact: She produced and starred in Barbie, which grossed over $1.4 billion to become the highest-grossing film ever directed by a woman.

person.name: Emma Stone
person.fact: She taught herself to play drums for a key scene in La La Land and won her second Oscar for a film in which she plays multiple versions of the same character across different realities.

person.name: Emma Watson
person.fact: She turned down multiple major roles to complete her degree at Brown University, becoming one of the few blockbuster child stars to emerge from their franchise with both an education and their dignity intact.

person.name: Daniel Radcliffe
person.fact: He wore the same outfit every day for months to foil paparazzi photographers trying to sell shots of him, and has since built a remarkably varied indie film career far from his wand.

person.name: Rupert Grint
person.fact: He is so rarely recognized without his signature red hair that he once successfully went undetected at a Harry Potter exhibition, queuing with regular fans for over an hour.

person.name: Kristen Stewart
person.fact: She was the first American actress to win a César Award, France's equivalent of the Oscar, for her role in Clouds of Sils Maria, silencing critics who had only known her from Twilight.

person.name: Robert Pattinson
person.fact: He was cast as a sparkly vampire largely on the strength of his performance in Harry Potter, then spent years doing experimental arthouse films to distance himself from the role before landing Batman.

person.name: Mila Kunis
person.fact: She lied about her age at her audition for That '70s Show, she was 14 when the role required 18, and kept the job for the entire run of the series.

person.name: Ashton Kutcher
person.fact: He was discovered at a bar in Iowa by a model scout while attending college on a biochemical engineering scholarship, then became one of the first celebrities to reach one million Twitter followers.

person.name: Mark Wahlberg
person.fact: He was a convicted criminal at 16 and a Calvin Klein underwear model at 20 before reinventing himself as a serious dramatic actor opposite the likes of Jack Nicholson.

person.name: Matthew McConaughey
person.fact: After years of formulaic rom-coms, he went on a deliberate hiatus, lost 47 pounds to play an AIDS patient, and won an Oscar, a career pivot that became known as the McConaissance.

person.name: Woody Harrelson
person.fact: He became famous playing a dim-witted barman, but his most daring performance may be True Detective, where he held his own against a co-star who famously stayed in character for eight months.

person.name: Owen Wilson
person.fact: His distinctive broken nose, the result of two separate incidents, became so much a part of his persona that he has never had it fixed, and his improvisational warmth made him one of comedy's most bankable stars.

person.name: Vince Vaughn
person.fact: His rapid-fire, improvisational delivery in Swingers, a micro-budget film shot in secret at real Hollywood venues, made him a star and created the template for a decade of bro comedies.

person.name: Will Ferrell
person.fact: His portrayal of a clueless elf who discovers he's human launched a Christmas movie franchise, but his most daring move was performing an entire Broadway show in character as his fictional country singer alter ego.

person.name: Jack Black
person.fact: He formed the comedy rock band Tenacious D with Kyle Gass in 1994 and has maintained the side project, with genuine albums and tours, in parallel with a major Hollywood career for three decades.

person.name: Seth Rogen
person.fact: He started doing stand-up comedy at 13 in Vancouver, was hired to write for Judd Apatow at 19, and helped define a generation of raunchy-but-heartfelt comedies that dominated the 2000s.

person.name: Steve Carell
person.fact: His departure from The Office was kept so secret that most of the cast didn't know until they filmed the episode, producing genuinely tearful reactions that directors used in the final cut.

person.name: Tina Fey
person.fact: She was the first female head writer in Saturday Night Live's history, created 30 Rock around her own experiences there, and wrote Mean Girls: all while critics debated whether women could be funny.

person.name: Amy Poehler
person.fact: She played the same optimistic government employee for seven seasons on Parks and Recreation, a character so beloved that fans genuinely mourned the show's end as if a real person had left their lives.

person.name: Melissa McCarthy
person.fact: She was rejected from Saturday Night Live, went on to co-write and star in Bridesmaids, received an Oscar nomination for it, and later hosted the very show that had turned her down.

person.name: Kristen Wiig
person.fact: She joined SNL as a featured player and quietly became its most versatile performer, with over 100 original characters, before leaving to make Bridesmaids and redefine the Hollywood comedy landscape for women.

person.name: Mindy Kaling
person.fact: She was the only woman and only person of color writing on The Office, where she also played a character, and went on to create, write, produce, and star in her own network show simultaneously.

person.name: Steven Spielberg
person.fact: He was rejected by USC's film school twice, snuck onto the Universal Studios lot to observe productions, and eventually talked his way into a directing deal: before making Jaws, which invented the summer blockbuster.

person.name: Martin Scorsese
person.fact: He has been nominated for the Best Director Oscar eight times and won once, for The Departed, despite being considered one of the greatest filmmakers who ever lived.

person.name: Quentin Tarantino
person.fact: He wrote the screenplays for Pulp Fiction and True Romance while working in a video rental store, drawing on an encyclopedic knowledge of cinema absorbed entirely outside of any film school.

person.name: Christopher Nolan
person.fact: He shot his debut feature Memento over weekends for under $10,000, then used it to convince studios to hand him the Batman franchise, and eventually a blank cheque for original blockbuster ideas.

person.name: James Cameron
person.fact: He was a truck driver before teaching himself filmmaking, and twice held the record for the highest-grossing film of all time: first with Titanic, then with Avatar.

person.name: George Lucas
person.fact: He sold the Star Wars merchandising rights to himself rather than the studio in lieu of a higher directing fee, a decision worth billions that changed how Hollywood thought about intellectual property forever.

person.name: Stanley Kubrick
