person.fact: He was so obsessed with perfection that he filmed one scene in The Shining 127 times, and kept the specific lens he used for Barry Lyndon a closely guarded secret for years.

person.name: Francis Ford Coppola
person.fact: He was nearly fired from The Godfather multiple times during production, but the finished film went on to win three Oscars and redefine what American cinema could be.

person.name: Tim Burton
person.fact: He was fired from Disney as an animator because his drawings were considered too dark and unsettling, then returned as one of Hollywood's most commercially successful directors of dark, unsettling films.

person.name: Wes Anderson
person.fact: His films are so visually distinctive, centered compositions, pastel palettes, precisely symmetrical frames, that his aesthetic has been parodied in hundreds of internet videos and even a fake AI-generated trailer.

person.name: Spike Lee
person.fact: He financed She's Gotta Have It by maxing out credit cards and calling in every favor he had in Brooklyn, then turned the film's success into a platform for unflinching commentary on race in America.

person.name: Peter Jackson
person.fact: He made his name with gleefully gory low-budget horror comedies in New Zealand before convincing a studio to let him film all three Lord of the Rings movies simultaneously.

person.name: Ron Howard
person.fact: He grew up on screen as a child actor, stepped behind the camera, and won the Best Director Oscar for A Beautiful Mind: one of the few people to succeed fully on both sides of the lens.

person.name: Woody Allen
person.fact: He wrote, directed, and starred in a film almost every single year for five decades, a prolific output that also includes multiple Grammy-winning comedy albums recorded before he ever made a movie.

person.name: Jordan Peele
person.fact: He spent a decade as one half of a sketch comedy duo before writing and directing Get Out, which won him the Oscar for Best Original Screenplay: the first Black writer to win that award solo.

person.name: Greta Gerwig
person.fact: Her Lady Bird was the first solo-directed film by a woman to receive a Best Picture Oscar nomination, then her Barbie smashed box office records while sparking a global cultural conversation.

person.name: Sofia Coppola
person.fact: Growing up on her father's film sets, she absorbed filmmaking by osmosis, then made Lost in Translation, a film so understated it earned her an Oscar for Best Original Screenplay.

person.name: J.J. Abrams
person.fact: He created a mystery-box approach to storytelling so influential, seeding unresolvable questions to keep audiences hooked, that it became both a filmmaking strategy and a cultural critique.

person.name: Guillermo del Toro
person.fact: He kept a notebook of ideas and sketches for decades before turning them into films: the notebook itself was displayed as an art object when it was eventually published.

person.name: Hayao Miyazaki
person.fact: He has retired and un-retired multiple times, but each farewell seemed to produce another masterpiece, including The Boy and the Heron, made in his eighties, which won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature.

person.name: Beyoncé
person.fact: She performed at the Super Bowl halftime show while secretly pregnant, and her visual album Lemonade, released as a complete surprise, redefined what a pop album release could be.

person.name: Jay-Z
person.fact: He built a business empire worth over $2.5 billion spanning music, streaming, sports management, and alcohol, becoming the first rapper to become a billionaire.

person.name: Rihanna
person.fact: She turned a Super Bowl halftime performance into a pregnancy announcement watched by 120 million people, and her Fenty Beauty line, built on radical shade inclusivity, revolutionized the cosmetics industry.

person.name: Taylor Swift
person.fact: She re-recorded her first six albums to reclaim ownership of her music after a catalogue dispute, turning a legal strategy into a pop cultural movement that sent old songs back to number one.

person.name: Adele
person.fact: Her album 21 spent 24 non-consecutive weeks at number one in the United States and sold over 30 million copies worldwide, making it one of the best-selling albums of the 21st century.

person.name: Lady Gaga
person.fact: She arrived at the 2010 VMAs in a dress made entirely of raw meat, but also composed and performed a Broadway-quality score for A Star Is Born, earning an Oscar for Best Original Song.

person.name: Katy Perry
person.fact: She became the first artist to have five number-one singles from a single album, Teenage Dream, matching a record previously held only by Michael Jackson.

person.name: Ariana Grande
person.fact: Her whistle-register notes are among the highest ever recorded in pop, and her ponytail became so iconic that costume shops stock it as a standalone Halloween accessory.

person.name: Billie Eilish
person.fact: She recorded her debut album in her brother's childhood bedroom, became the first person born in the 2000s to top the charts, and won all four major Grammy categories in a single night at age 18.

person.name: Dua Lipa
person.fact: Her album Future Nostalgia was recorded before the pandemic and released into lockdown, where its disco-inflected escapism became the soundtrack to a world stuck indoors.

person.name: Olivia Rodrigo
person.fact: She wrote 'drivers license' about a heartbreak and released it as a debut single: it shattered Spotify's single-day streaming record and spent eight weeks at number one before she had a full album out.

person.name: Sabrina Carpenter
person.fact: She spent years as a Disney Channel regular before 'Espresso', a fizzy, self-penned earworm, went globally viral in 2024 and made her one of pop's biggest breakout stars.

person.name: Miley Cyrus
person.fact: She performed a notorious twerking routine at the VMAs that generated more tweets per minute than any previous event, then followed it with an album that revealed a genuinely powerful rock voice.

person.name: Selena Gomez
person.fact: She quietly became the most-followed person on Instagram for years while managing a kidney transplant in private, and her candid discussions of mental health helped shift public conversation around celebrity wellness.

person.name: Demi Lovato
person.fact: She publicly documented her struggles with eating disorders and addiction in a documentary series, turning her own story into a platform for mental health advocacy long before it was a mainstream celebrity cause.

person.name: Christina Aguilera
person.fact: She was cast alongside Britney Spears on The Mickey Mouse Club as a child, then out-vocalized virtually every contemporary with a five-octave range that earned her the nickname 'the Voice'.

person.name: Britney Spears
person.fact: The opening bars of '...Baby One More Time' are among the most recognizable in pop history, and her 2007 public breakdown, and the conservatorship that followed, sparked a worldwide Free Britney movement years later.

person.name: Jennifer Lopez
person.fact: She was paid $1 million for Selena in 1997, making her the first Latina actress to earn that figure, then crossed over into music and became simultaneously one of Hollywood's biggest stars and pop's most durable hitmakers.

person.name: Mariah Carey
person.fact: She owns Christmas: her 1994 song 'All I Want for Christmas Is You' returns to number one every December, making her the first artist in history to top the charts in four separate decades with a single song.

person.name: Céline Dion
person.fact: She sang 'My Heart Will Go On' in a single take and reportedly disliked it, but it became the best-selling physical single by a female artist in history and the signature song of the 1990s.

person.name: Shakira
person.fact: She taught herself to play guitar as a child in Colombia and co-wrote most of her own material in two languages: her hips and her honesty both became internationally recognized cultural touchstones.

person.name: Pink
person.fact: She has performed some of the most dangerous aerial acrobatics in pop concert history, including suspended harness routines in pouring rain, doing them live, without a safety net of lip-syncing.

person.name: Alicia Keys
person.fact: She graduated high school at 16 as valedictorian while simultaneously recording her debut album, which debuted at number one and made her the best-selling new artist of 2001.

person.name: John Legend
person.fact: He is one of the few people to have achieved EGOT status, winning an Emmy, Grammy, Oscar, and Tony, and he completed the set at just 39 years old.

person.name: Bruno Mars
person.fact: He grew up performing in a family band in Hawaii, impersonating Elvis at age four, and later became one of the last major pop artists to write, produce, and perform virtually all of his own material.

person.name: Justin Bieber
person.fact: He was discovered at 13 via YouTube videos his mother posted, making him the first major pop star whose career was entirely launched by social media rather than a traditional record deal.

person.name: Justin Timberlake
person.fact: He ripped Janet Jackson's costume at the Super Bowl halftime show in 2004, an incident that ignited a national debate about media double standards; Jackson's career suffered while his continued to soar.

person.name: Ed Sheeran
person.fact: He slept on the London Underground and on friends' sofas for a year while trying to break through, before a chance connection with Jamie Foxx led to his first big break.

person.name: Harry Styles
person.fact: He auditioned as a solo act on The X Factor, was eliminated, then was placed into a group with four other rejected soloists, a decision that created one of the best-selling boy bands in history.

person.name: Shawn Mendes
person.fact: He built a following by posting six-second cover videos on Vine as a teenager, becoming one of the first musicians to convert a social media presence directly into a record deal and a number-one album.

person.name: The Weeknd
person.fact: He uploaded his first three mixtapes anonymously to YouTube with no press, no label, and no real name attached, amassing millions of streams before anyone knew who he was.

person.name: Drake
person.fact: He was best known as a teenage wheelchair user on a Canadian high school drama before reinventing himself as a rapper and becoming one of the most-streamed artists in Spotify's history.

person.name: Kanye West
person.fact: He was told by nearly every major label that a producer couldn't be a rapper, so he famously recorded vocals for 'Through the Wire' with his jaw wired shut after a near-fatal car accident.

person.name: Kendrick Lamar
person.fact: His album To Pimp a Butterfly was the first rap album to win the Pulitzer Prize for Music, and his Super Bowl halftime show in 2025 became one of the most discussed in the event's history.

person.name: Eminem
person.fact: He was a white rapper from Detroit who was told he'd never make it in hip-hop, then released The Slim Shady LP and sold over 1.8 million copies in its first week.

person.name: Snoop Dogg
person.fact: He was arrested on murder charges the same week his debut album Doggystyle was released, the charges were eventually dropped, and the album went on to sell four million copies in its first year.

person.name: Dr. Dre
person.fact: He produced both Snoop Dogg's and Eminem's debut albums, effectively launching two of the biggest careers in hip-hop history, then sold his Beats Electronics company to Apple for $3 billion.

person.name: 50 Cent
person.fact: He was shot nine times on his doorstep in 2000, survived, was dropped by his label, and used the story as the foundation of a mixtape buzz campaign that led to one of the best-selling debut albums of the decade.

person.name: Tupac Shakur
person.fact: He recorded so prolifically that new albums were released for years after his death, and his hologram appeared onstage at Coachella in 2012, reigniting debates about artistic legacy and whether he had faked his death.

person.name: The Notorious B.I.G.
person.fact: He grew up selling crack on a Brooklyn street corner, taught himself to freestyle, and within a few years had crafted Ready to Die, considered one of the greatest debut albums in the history of hip-hop.

person.name: Lil Wayne
person.fact: He released 'Tha Carter III' in 2008, which debuted at #1 and sold over a million copies in its first week, cementing him as the self-proclaimed 'Best Rapper Alive.'

person.name: Travis Scott
person.fact: His Astroworld album turned a Houston fairground nostalgia trip into a cultural phenomenon, complete with a rollercoaster named after him at the actual Six Flags.

person.name: Post Malone
person.fact: He went from a Dallas teenager recording in his friend's closet to having 'Congratulations' rack up billions of streams before his debut album even dropped.

person.name: Cardi B
person.fact: She became the first solo female rapper to win the Grammy for Best Rap Album, taking it home for 'Invasion of Privacy' in 2019.

person.name: Nicki Minaj
person.fact: She held the record for the most entries on the Billboard Hot 100 by a female artist for years, thanks in part to a seemingly endless string of guest verses.

person.name: Megan Thee Stallion
person.fact: Her 'Savage (Remix)' with Beyoncé hit #1 in 2020 even as she was finishing her degree in health administration at Texas Southern University.

person.name: Ice Cube
person.fact: As a founding member of N.W.A, he wrote most of the lyrics for 'Straight Outta Compton,' the album that scared the FBI enough to send the group an official warning letter.

person.name: LL Cool J
person.fact: He released 'Rock the Bells' at 16 and became one of the first rappers to prove the genre could sustain a decades-long career; his initials stand for 'Ladies Love Cool James.'

person.name: Pharrell Williams
person.fact: He and his Neptunes partner Chad Hugo won Producer of the Year at the Grammys twice, and his song 'Happy' from Despicable Me 2 became one of the best-selling singles of all time.

person.name: Usher
person.fact: His 2004 album 'Confessions' spent 11 weeks at #1 and sold over 20 million copies, and he discovered and mentored Justin Bieber as a teenager.

person.name: Chris Brown
person.fact: At 16 he became the first male artist since Stevie Wonder to debut at #1 with a debut single, and he is widely considered one of the most technically skilled dancers in pop history.

person.name: Frank Ocean
person.fact: He released 'Channel Orange' after a Tumblr post quietly disclosing a past relationship with a man, a rare, understated coming-out that reshaped conversations about queerness in R&B.

person.name: Bad Bunny
person.fact: The Puerto Rican trap star became the most-streamed artist on Spotify globally three years in a row (2020-2022), the first non-English-language act to achieve that.

person.name: Doja Cat
person.fact: Her song 'Say So' went viral via TikTok before being officially released, a textbook case of internet-era music stardom that she followed with a Nicki Minaj remix that sent it to #1.

person.name: Lana Del Rey
person.fact: Her breakout video 'Video Games', shot on a flip camera and self-uploaded to YouTube in 2011, went viral and landed her a major-label deal almost overnight.

person.name: Bruce Springsteen
person.fact: His 1975 album 'Born to Run' earned him simultaneous covers of Time and Newsweek, and his legendary live shows often run four hours, making him synonymous with rock as communal endurance.

person.name: Mick Jagger
person.fact: As the Rolling Stones' frontman since 1962, he has performed across six decades and reportedly turned down a knighthood once before eventually accepting Sir Mick status in 2003.

person.name: Keith Richards
person.fact: He wrote 'Satisfaction' in his sleep, literally woke up, recorded the riff, and fell back asleep, and his lifestyle inspired Johnny Depp's portrayal of Captain Jack Sparrow.

person.name: Ringo Starr
person.fact: Despite being the butt of 'worst Beatle' jokes, he was voted the greatest rock drummer of all time by Rolling Stone readers in 2011, with legends like Dave Grohl citing his feel.

person.name: George Harrison
person.fact: Often called 'the quiet Beatle,' he wrote 'Something,' which Frank Sinatra called the greatest love song of 50 years, and organized the first major rock benefit concert in 1971.

person.name: Eric Clapton
person.fact: 1960s London fans spray-painted 'Clapton is God' on Underground walls, and he is the only three-time inductee into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame: solo artist, Yardbird, and Cream member.

person.name: Billy Joel
person.fact: He played the final concert ever held at Shea Stadium in 2008, closing with 'Piano Man', a song he wrote while playing piano under a pseudonym in an LA bar when his career had stalled.

person.name: Neil Young
person.fact: He earned the nickname 'the Godfather of Grunge' for his distorted guitar influence on a generation, and famously pulled his entire catalog from Spotify over a COVID misinformation dispute.

person.name: Joni Mitchell
person.fact: Her 1971 album 'Blue' is frequently ranked the greatest album ever made, and she invented over 50 open guitar tunings because childhood polio left her hand unable to form standard chord shapes.

person.name: Cher
person.fact: She has had number-one hits in six consecutive decades, a record no other solo artist holds, from 'I Got You Babe' in the 1960s to 'Believe' in the 1990s, the latter pioneering the Auto-Tune effect.

person.name: Diana Ross
person.fact: As lead of the Supremes she scored 12 number-one hits, more than any 1960s act except the Beatles, and was later nominated for an Oscar for playing Billie Holiday.

person.name: Ray Charles
person.fact: He fused gospel, blues, and country so controversially that Georgia initially banned him, before later naming 'Georgia on My Mind' the official state song.

person.name: Johnny Cash
person.fact: He performed at Folsom Prison in 1968 in a concert that revived his flagging career, and dressed entirely in black as a protest against poverty, turning a wardrobe choice into a political statement.

person.name: Willie Nelson
person.fact: He wrote 'Crazy', one of the most recorded songs in history, and sold it to Patsy Cline when his own unconventional vocal style couldn't get radio play.

person.name: Dolly Parton
person.fact: She secretly funded the Moderna COVID vaccine research and declined a state statue of herself, saying she hadn't earned it: while her song 'I Will Always Love You' was written as a business breakup letter to her mentor.

person.name: Garth Brooks
person.fact: He became the best-selling solo artist in U.S. history, outselling Elvis Presley, and his alter-ego 'Chris Gaines', a fictional rock star invented for a film project, remains one of pop culture's strangest career detours.

person.name: Shania Twain
person.fact: 'Come On Over' is the best-selling country album of all time and the best-selling album by any female artist in any genre, with over 40 million copies sold.

person.name: Carrie Underwood
person.fact: She won American Idol in 2005 and became its commercially most successful contestant, and her debut single 'Before He Cheats' spent 28 weeks at number one: the longest run by a debut country single.

person.name: Luke Bryan
person.fact: He racked up more number-one singles on the Billboard Country Airplay chart than any other artist in the 2010s, and took over guardianship of his nephew and niece after losing both his brother and sister-in-law.

person.name: Reba McEntire
person.fact: She survived the worst disaster in country music history when seven band members died in a 1991 plane crash, returned to touring within months, and later starred in a sitcom that ran for six seasons.

person.name: Faith Hill
person.fact: Her 'Breathe' album sold over 8 million copies and made her one of the few country artists to cross over to mainstream pop in the late 1990s.

person.name: Tim McGraw
person.fact: His 'Live Like You Were Dying' won the Grammy for Best Country Song in 2005, and he proposed to Faith Hill while on tour together in 1996.

person.name: Morgan Wallen
person.fact: His double album 'Dangerous' spent a record 10 consecutive weeks at number one on the Billboard 200, the longest streak for any country album.

person.name: Lizzo
person.fact: She played James Madison's 200-year-old crystal flute, borrowed from the Library of Congress, during a sold-out Washington D.C. show, the first time the instrument had been played in over a century.

person.name: Lorde
person.fact: She was 16 when 'Royals' became the first solo female artist born in the 1990s to top the Billboard Hot 100, a wry critique of hip-hop wealth fantasies from a suburban Auckland teenager.

person.name: Halsey
person.fact: She self-released music on SoundCloud as a teenager and built a fanbase before she had a record deal, eventually headlining arenas and releasing albums that spanned every genre.

person.name: Sia
person.fact: She hid her face behind a giant bow-and-wig costume for years, and her 'Chandelier' music video featuring dancer Maddie Ziegler has over a billion YouTube views.

person.name: Amy Winehouse
person.fact: Her album 'Back to Black' won five Grammy Awards in 2008, including Record, Song, and Album of the Year, a record for a British female artist that stood for years.

person.name: Pink Floyd's Roger Waters
person.fact: He conceived 'The Wall' as a metaphor for his own emotional alienation, and the 1980-81 tour featured an actual 40-foot wall built across the stage mid-show.

person.name: Ozzy Osbourne
person.fact: He was fired from Black Sabbath and proceeded to have a bigger solo career, and became tabloid legend when he bit the head off a bat thrown onstage at a 1982 concert.

person.name: Axl Rose
person.fact: Guns N' Roses' 'Appetite for Destruction' is the best-selling debut album in U.S. history, with over 30 million copies sold worldwide.

person.name: Slash
person.fact: His top-hat-and-cigarette silhouette is one of rock's most recognizable icons, and the opening riff of 'Sweet Child O' Mine', written in under a minute as a warmup exercise, became one of the most-played guitar riffs ever.

person.name: Bono
person.fact: He negotiated directly with world leaders as part of his ONE Campaign, securing billions in aid for Africa, and U2's 'The Joshua Tree' made the Irish band the biggest rock act in the world in 1987.

person.name: Sting
person.fact: After leaving The Police at their commercial peak, he spent years advocating for Amazon rainforest preservation and his claim of tantric discipline became a cultural punchline that somehow only increased his mystique.

person.name: Phil Collins
person.fact: He played Live Aid twice in 1985, once in London and once in Philadelphia the same day, after flying across the Atlantic on Concorde, and his 'In the Air Tonight' drum fill is one of the most air-drummed moments in pop history.

person.name: Lenny Kravitz
person.fact: He wrote, produced, and played nearly every instrument on his debut album alone in a New York apartment, and won four consecutive Grammys for Best Male Rock Vocal Performance, a record that still stands.

person.name: Carlos Santana
person.fact: His Woodstock performance in 1969 is one of rock history's most celebrated sets, and thirty years later his 'Supernatural' album won nine Grammys in a single night.

person.name: Quincy Jones
person.fact: He produced Michael Jackson's 'Thriller,' 'Off the Wall,' and 'Bad', three of the best-selling albums ever, and with 28 Grammy Awards is among the most decorated individuals in the ceremony's history.

person.name: Pavarotti
person.fact: His 1990 performance of 'Nessun Dorma' at the BBC's World Cup broadcast introduced opera to hundreds of millions of TV viewers, making the aria a sporting-event staple.

person.name: Andrea Bocelli
person.fact: Blind since the age of 12, he has sold over 90 million records, more than any classical or operatic artist in history, with a crossover career that kept him atop both classical and pop charts.

person.name: Yo-Yo Ma
person.fact: He founded the Silk Road Ensemble blending Central Asian, Chinese, and Western musical traditions, and played at Barack Obama's inauguration in 2009 in temperatures so cold the performance had to be pre-recorded.

person.name: Lin-Manuel Miranda
person.fact: He conceived 'Hamilton' while reading a biography on vacation and performed the first song at the White House for President Obama before the show won 11 Tonys.

person.name: Andrew Lloyd Webber
person.fact: He composed 'The Phantom of the Opera,' the longest-running show in Broadway history, which ran for 35 years, and co-wrote 'Jesus Christ Superstar' with Tim Rice before age 25.

person.name: Hans Zimmer
person.fact: He scored 'The Lion King,' 'Gladiator,' 'Inception,' and 'Interstellar,' pioneering non-Western instruments and electronic synthesis in blockbuster film scores.

person.name: John Williams
person.fact: He composed the themes for 'Star Wars,' 'Indiana Jones,' 'Jaws,' and 'Harry Potter', a run so ubiquitous that a single note can identify each film, and holds 54 Academy Award nominations.

person.name: Ludwig van Beethoven
person.fact: He composed his Ninth Symphony, including the 'Ode to Joy', after he was completely deaf, and famously turned around at the premiere to see the standing ovation he couldn't hear.

person.name: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart
person.fact: He composed his first symphony at age 8 and over 600 works before dying at 35, and his 'Don Giovanni' overture was reportedly handed to musicians as the audience was still taking their seats.

person.name: Johann Sebastian Bach
person.fact: He fathered 20 children and still produced over 1,000 compositions, but his music was largely forgotten after his death until Felix Mendelssohn revived the 'St. Matthew Passion' in 1829.

person.name: Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky
person.fact: His ballets; 'Swan Lake,' 'The Nutcracker,' and 'Sleeping Beauty': defined classical ballet for Western audiences, and his 1812 Overture famously calls for real cannon fire.

person.name: Frédéric Chopin
person.fact: He performed publicly only about 30 times in his entire life, preferring salon settings to concert halls, yet his études and nocturnes became the foundation of modern piano technique.

person.name: Antonio Vivaldi
person.fact: He wrote over 500 concertos and taught at a Venetian orphanage whose all-girl orchestra became one of the most celebrated in Europe.

person.name: Kobe Bryant
person.fact: He scored 81 points in a single NBA game in 2006, the second-highest individual scoring game in history, and won an Academy Award for Best Animated Short Film in 2018.

person.name: Shaquille O'Neal
person.fact: His combination of 7-foot-1 height and 325 pounds made him arguably the most physically dominant player the NBA has ever seen, and he also had a platinum-selling rap single while still an active player.

person.name: Magic Johnson
person.fact: His 6-foot-9 point guard frame broke every assumption about the position, and his 1991 announcement that he had contracted HIV, at a time of enormous stigma, changed public understanding of the disease.

person.name: Larry Bird
person.fact: He won three consecutive NBA MVP awards and made Celtics-Lakers a cultural rivalry that helped save the NBA from low ratings and tape-delayed Finals coverage.

person.name: Kareem Abdul-Jabbar
person.fact: His skyhook, an unstoppable one-handed shot no defense ever solved, remained effective across a 20-year career, and he held the NBA's all-time scoring record from 1984 until LeBron James surpassed it.

person.name: Stephen Curry
person.fact: He revolutionized basketball by making the three-pointer the central offensive weapon, and his 2016 MVP was the first unanimous vote in NBA history.

person.name: Kevin Durant
person.fact: His 7-foot frame with a shooting guard's skill set made him a position-less player before the term was common, and his decision to join the Golden State Warriors sparked one of the most polarizing debates in modern sports.

person.name: Giannis Antetokounmpo
person.fact: He went from selling trinkets on the streets of Athens as a teenager to winning two NBA MVP awards and an NBA championship before turning 27, earning the nickname 'The Greek Freak.'

person.name: Allen Iverson
person.fact: He was the first player under 6-foot-1 to win an NBA MVP award, and his crossover dribble, cornrow braids, and tattoos made him the avatar of a generation's basketball identity.

person.name: Charles Barkley
person.fact: Listed at 6-foot-4, he dominated players much taller through sheer physicality, and his Nike ad declaring 'I am not a role model' became one of sports history's most debated statements.

person.name: Dennis Rodman
person.fact: He led the league in rebounding seven consecutive seasons and won five championships, while also making solo diplomatic trips to North Korea and developing a personal friendship with Kim Jong-un.

person.name: Caitlin Clark
person.fact: She became the all-time leading scorer in NCAA Division I basketball history, men's or women's, breaking Pete Maravich's 53-year-old record, and her move to the WNBA shattered the league's viewership records.

person.name: Tom Brady
person.fact: He was the 199th pick in the 2000 NFL Draft, taken after six other quarterbacks, and went on to win seven Super Bowl rings, more than any franchise in history.

person.name: Peyton Manning
person.fact: He was the first player to win Super Bowls with two different franchises as starting quarterback, and his forehead-slapping, audible-calling offense at the line of scrimmage changed how the entire position operates.

person.name: Patrick Mahomes
person.fact: By age 28 he had already won four Super Bowl MVP awards, and his sidearm and no-look passes have expanded the vocabulary of what a quarterback throw can look like.

person.name: Aaron Rodgers
person.fact: His Hail Mary to beat Dallas in 2016, thrown off his back foot as he was being tackled, is one of the most replayed moments in NFL playoff history.

person.name: Joe Montana
person.fact: He never threw an interception in four Super Bowl appearances, going 4-0, and his final-drive against Cincinnati with 3:10 left and no timeouts is still considered the greatest clutch performance in NFL history.

person.name: Jerry Rice
person.fact: He holds virtually every significant NFL receiving record by margins considered unbreakable, and ran routes so precisely that defensive backs said they could set a watch by his cuts.

person.name: Brett Favre
person.fact: He started 297 consecutive regular-season games, a record for any position player, and his gunslinger style produced three consecutive MVP awards alongside some of the most memorable interceptions ever thrown.

person.name: Travis Kelce
person.fact: He is the most productive tight end in NFL postseason history, and his relationship with Taylor Swift in 2023 turned his already high profile into a cross-cultural phenomenon.

person.name: O.J. Simpson
person.fact: He was the first running back to rush for 2,000 yards in a single NFL season (1973), and his 1995 murder trial became one of the most watched televised events in American history.

person.name: David Beckham
person.fact: He scored from the halfway line on the Premier League's opening day of 1996, a 57-yard lob that became one of football's most iconic goals, and was the first British player to win league titles in four different countries.

person.name: Zinedine Zidane
person.fact: His volley in the 2002 Champions League Final is consistently voted the greatest goal in the competition's history, and he led France to both the World Cup and European Championship in back-to-back years.

person.name: Ronaldinho
person.fact: He scored two curling free-kicks against Real Madrid and received a standing ovation from the opposing Bernabeu crowd: one of the rarest moments in football history.

person.name: Ronaldo Nazário
person.fact: He scored twice in the 2002 World Cup Final after two years barely playing due to mysterious seizures, and his dominance was so total that 'Ronaldo' had to become 'CR7' just to distinguish a later player.

person.name: Neymar
person.fact: He became the world's most expensive footballer at 222 million euros in 2017, and was the fulcrum of the MSN trio with Messi and Suarez at Barcelona that won the treble in 2015.

person.name: Kylian Mbappé
person.fact: He became only the second teenager in history, after Pelé, to score in a World Cup Final, doing so in France's 2018 triumph at age 19.

person.name: Mohamed Salah
person.fact: He scored 32 Premier League goals in 2017-18, breaking the record for a 38-game season, and has been notably vocal about being a Muslim in European football.

person.name: Megan Rapinoe
person.fact: She scored two goals in the 2019 Women's World Cup Final and won both the Golden Boot and Golden Ball in the same tournament, and was the first white athlete and first woman to kneel during the national anthem.

person.name: Mia Hamm
person.fact: She was the world's all-time leading goal scorer in international soccer, men's or women's, at the time of her retirement, and was the first female footballer to become a mainstream American sports icon.

person.name: Rafael Nadal
person.fact: He won the French Open 14 times, a number so surreal that the main court at Roland Garros was renamed in his honor while he was still winning on it.

person.name: Novak Djokovic
person.fact: He holds the record for the most Grand Slam singles titles in men's tennis history, and was deported from Australia before the 2022 Open for refusing COVID vaccination, turning a sports story into a global political flashpoint.

person.name: Venus Williams
person.fact: She was the first Black woman ranked number one in the Open Era, and her campaign alongside her family to force equal prize money at Wimbledon succeeded in 2007.

person.name: Billie Jean King
person.fact: She beat Bobby Riggs in the 'Battle of the Sexes' in 1973 before 90 million TV viewers, a match that became a landmark moment for gender equality in sports.

person.name: Naomi Osaka
person.fact: She won four Major titles before turning 24, and her 2021 French Open withdrawal, disclosing depression and anxiety, ignited a global conversation about athlete mental health.

person.name: Carlos Alcaraz
person.fact: He became the youngest player in history to reach the ATP world number one ranking at 19, and his game draws comparisons to Federer, Nadal, and Djokovic simultaneously.

person.name: Jack Nicklaus
person.fact: He won 18 Major championships, a record considered the sport's ultimate benchmark, and designed over 300 golf courses worldwide after retiring.

person.name: Arnold Palmer
person.fact: He transformed golf from a country-club pursuit into a televised mass sport in the 1960s, and the drink named after him, half iced tea, half lemonade, appears on menus worldwide.

person.name: Rory McIlroy
person.fact: He won four Major championships before turning 26, and has been the central figure in the PGA Tour vs. LIV Golf conflict, choosing to stay with the established tour when many peers defected.

person.name: Sidney Crosby
person.fact: He was selected first overall in 2005: the year the NHL cancelled its entire season, known as the 'Crosby Sweepstakes', and his overtime goal in the 2010 Olympics gold medal game is the most-watched moment in Canadian sports TV history.

person.name: Alexander Ovechkin
person.fact: He broke Wayne Gretzky's all-time NHL goal-scoring record in 2023, a mark that had stood for 30 years, and has led the league in goals scored nine times.

person.name: Mario Lemieux
person.fact: He scored a goal in five different ways in a single game in 1988, a feat never replicated, and later bought the Pittsburgh Penguins to save them from bankruptcy, then came out of retirement to play for the team he owned.

person.name: Derek Jeter
person.fact: He made 'The Flip', a no-look shovel pass to nail Jeremy Giambi at the plate in the 2001 ALDS: one of the most instinctive defensive plays in playoff history, and his 3,000th hit was a home run.

person.name: Mickey Mantle
person.fact: He hit a home run in 1953 measured at 565 feet, coining the term 'tape-measure home run', and is considered the greatest what-if player in baseball history due to chronic knee injuries.

person.name: Hank Aaron
person.fact: He held baseball's all-time home run record for 33 years, breaking Babe Ruth's mark in 1974 while receiving death threats, and preserved the racist hate mail he received to donate to the Baseball Hall of Fame.

person.name: Willie Mays
person.fact: His over-the-shoulder catch in the 1954 World Series, sprinting with his back to the plate to snag a 425-foot drive, is simply called 'The Catch' and is considered the greatest defensive play in baseball history.

person.name: Joe DiMaggio
person.fact: He hit safely in 56 consecutive games in 1941, a record statisticians rank among the most improbable achievements in all of sports, and sent roses to Marilyn Monroe's grave three times a week for 20 years after her death.

person.name: Barry Bonds
person.fact: His 2001 season of 73 home runs and a .515 on-base percentage is statistically the greatest offensive season in baseball history, and he was intentionally walked 120 times in 2004 as managers simply refused to pitch to him.

person.name: Shohei Ohtani
person.fact: He is the only player in MLB history to be a legitimate ace starter and a legitimate cleanup hitter simultaneously, and in 2023 signed the largest contract in sports history, $700 million over 10 years, with the Los Angeles Dodgers.

person.name: Mike Trout
person.fact: Widely regarded as the best baseball player of his generation, he won three American League MVP awards by age 27 while playing his entire career for the Los Angeles Angels.

person.name: Floyd Mayweather Jr.
person.fact: Retired with a perfect 50-0 professional record, his 2015 fight against Manny Pacquiao became the highest-grossing boxing match in history at the time.

person.name: Manny Pacquiao
person.fact: The only boxer to win world titles in eight different weight divisions, he later served as a senator and presidential candidate in the Philippines.

person.name: George Foreman
person.fact: He lost the 'Rumble in the Jungle' to Muhammad Ali in 1974, then made a stunning comeback to reclaim the heavyweight title at age 45, and later sold more than 100 million George Foreman Grills.

person.name: Evander Holyfield
person.fact: A four-time heavyweight champion best remembered for two brutal fights with Mike Tyson, the second of which ended when Tyson infamously bit off a chunk of his ear.

person.name: Conor McGregor
person.fact: The first UFC fighter to hold titles in two weight classes simultaneously, he crossed over to boxing in 2017 for a mega-fight against Floyd Mayweather that generated over $600 million.

person.name: Ronda Rousey
person.fact: She dominated women's MMA so thoroughly that her first 12 UFC fights lasted a combined total of under 15 minutes, and she is widely credited with making women's MMA mainstream.

person.name: Lewis Hamilton
person.fact: He matched and then surpassed Michael Schumacher's all-time record of seven Formula 1 World Championship titles, becoming the sport's most decorated driver in history.

person.name: Dale Earnhardt
person.fact: Known as 'The Intimidator,' he won seven NASCAR Cup Series championships but died tragically in a last-lap crash at the 2001 Daytona 500, sending shockwaves through the sport.

person.name: Max Verstappen
person.fact: He clinched his first Formula 1 championship in 2021 on the final lap of the final race in controversial circumstances, then dominated the sport with a record-breaking 19 wins in a single season.

person.name: Michael Phelps
person.fact: The most decorated Olympian of all time with 23 gold medals, he became a legend at the 2008 Beijing Games when he won eight gold medals in a single Olympics.

person.name: Katie Ledecky
person.fact: She has shattered her own world records in long-distance freestyle swimming multiple times, often winning races by margins that make her competitors look like they are in a different event.

person.name: Simone Biles
person.fact: Considered the greatest gymnast of all time, she has a signature vault named after her that is rated so dangerous the scoring code initially discouraged others from attempting it.

person.name: Nadia Comăneci
person.fact: At the 1976 Montreal Olympics she became the first gymnast ever to be awarded a perfect score of 10.0, a feat so unprecedented that the scoreboard could only display '1.00.'

person.name: Mary Lou Retton
person.fact: She vaulted into American hearts at the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics with a perfect 10 on her final vault, clinching the all-around gold medal by the slimmest possible margin.

person.name: Michelle Kwan
person.fact: One of the most beloved American figure skaters ever, she won nine U.S. championships and five World titles but famously never captured the Olympic gold medal that eluded her twice.

person.name: Tonya Harding
person.fact: Her career was derailed by the 1994 scandal in which her ex-husband arranged an attack on rival Nancy Kerrigan's knee before the U.S. Figure Skating Championships.

person.name: Shaun White
person.fact: Nicknamed 'The Flying Tomato' for his wild red hair, he won three Olympic halfpipe gold medals in snowboarding and is the only athlete to win gold at five consecutive X Games in a single discipline.

person.name: Tony Hawk
person.fact: He landed the first-ever documented 900, two-and-a-half aerial rotations on a skateboard, at the 1999 X Games, a trick many thought was physically impossible.

person.name: Lance Armstrong
person.fact: He won seven consecutive Tour de France titles from 1999 to 2005 after surviving testicular cancer, only to have all his titles stripped when he confessed to a systematic doping program.

person.name: Hulk Hogan
person.fact: The face of professional wrestling's 1980s boom, his signature leg drop finishing move and 'Hulkamania' persona helped transform the WWF into a global entertainment phenomenon.

person.name: John Cena
person.fact: He holds the record for the most Make-A-Wish Foundation wishes granted of any individual, fulfilling over 650 wishes, while also transitioning into a successful Hollywood acting career.

person.name: "Stone Cold" Steve Austin
person.fact: His beer-smashing, authority-defying 'Stone Cold' character in the late 1990s became the defining anti-hero of wrestling's Attitude Era and helped WWE win the Monday Night Wars.

person.name: MrBeast
person.fact: He built YouTube's most-subscribed individual channel through increasingly elaborate stunts and giveaways, including recreating the entire Squid Game set and giving away millions of dollars.

person.name: PewDiePie
person.fact: The Swedish gaming commentator was YouTube's most-subscribed individual creator for years, sparking a cultural moment when T-Series challenged his crown in a months-long subscriber race.

person.name: Logan Paul
person.fact: After a major controversy over a video filmed in Japan's Aokigahara forest, he rebranded by boxing Floyd Mayweather in an exhibition match and co-founding the Prime energy drink brand.

person.name: Jake Paul
person.fact: He parlayed his YouTube fame into a professional boxing career, defeating several former MMA champions and eventually fighting Mike Tyson in a Netflix-streamed event.

person.name: Markiplier
person.fact: Known for his hugely popular horror game playthroughs, he raised millions for charity through livestreams and later co-directed an interactive YouTube film with a branching choose-your-own-adventure format.

person.name: KSI
person.fact: The British YouTuber turned his rivalry with Logan Paul into two boxing matches that drew millions of pay-per-view buys and helped legitimize the YouTube-to-boxing pipeline.

person.name: Charli D'Amelio
person.fact: She became TikTok's most-followed creator as a teenager almost purely through dance videos, reaching 100 million followers faster than anyone else on the platform.

person.name: Addison Rae
person.fact: One of TikTok's earliest mega-stars, she leveraged her following into a Netflix film deal and a music career, becoming one of the highest-earning TikTok creators.

person.name: Khaby Lame
person.fact: The Senegalese-Italian creator became the most-followed person on TikTok without ever speaking, using deadpan reactions to mock needlessly complicated life-hack videos.

person.name: Bella Poarch
person.fact: Her slow head-bob lip-sync to 'M to the B' became the most-liked video in TikTok history at the time, catapulting her to fame before she pivoted to a music career.

person.name: Ninja
person.fact: He became the face of competitive Fortnite streaming, was the first gamer on the cover of ESPN Magazine, and once drew 635,000 concurrent viewers for a single stream with Drake.

person.name: Pokimane
person.fact: One of Twitch's most-watched female streamers, she co-founded the Offline TV content house and became a symbol of the mainstream crossover of gaming culture.

person.name: Dr Disrespect
person.fact: Known for his theatrical villain persona complete with mullet wig and wraparound shades, he was mysteriously and permanently banned from Twitch in 2020 with no official explanation ever given.

person.name: Kai Cenat
person.fact: He broke the record for most concurrent Twitch subscribers multiple times and became famous for marathon 'subathon' streams lasting weeks, cementing his status as the platform's biggest star.

person.name: Hasan Piker
person.fact: The nephew of The Young Turks founder Cenk Uygur, he became Twitch's most-watched political streamer by blending leftist commentary with gaming and pop culture.

person.name: Joe Rogan
person.fact: His long-form podcast became the world's most-listened-to podcast, drawing controversy when he signed an exclusive $200 million deal with Spotify and platformed guests with fringe views.

person.name: Lex Fridman
person.fact: The MIT researcher turned podcaster is known for marathon multi-hour interviews with scientists, world leaders, and tech figures, as well as his signature black suit worn in every episode.

person.name: Andrew Tate
person.fact: The former kickboxing world champion became one of the most-searched people on the internet in 2022 through viral misogynistic content, before being banned from major platforms and arrested in Romania.

person.name: Jordan Peterson
person.fact: His 12 Rules for Life sold millions of copies worldwide, and a viral 2018 Channel 4 interview in which he sparred with journalist Cathy Newman became one of the most-watched political interviews on YouTube.

person.name: Ellen DeGeneres
person.fact: She came out as gay in a 1997 episode of her sitcom in a landmark TV moment, later building a daytime talk show empire that ran for 19 seasons before ending amid workplace culture allegations.

person.name: Jimmy Fallon
person.fact: Host of The Tonight Show known for his celebrity games and musical impressions, he became famous for making his guests laugh during SNL sketches, often inadvertently.

person.name: Jimmy Kimmel
person.fact: The host of Jimmy Kimmel Live is known for his annual 'I Told My Kids I Ate Their Halloween Candy' viral segments and his emotional monologues on healthcare and gun control.

person.name: Stephen Colbert
person.fact: He spent nine years playing an exaggerated right-wing pundit on The Colbert Report before dropping the character to take over The Late Show, where his Trump-era monologues went massively viral.

person.name: Conan O'Brien
person.fact: After a very public falling-out with NBC over The Tonight Show that became a national story, he reinvented late night by leaning into self-deprecating absurdism and eventually launched a hit travel podcast.

person.name: David Letterman
person.fact: The host of Late Night and then the Late Show for over 30 years, he pioneered the modern talk show format and is famous for his gap-toothed wit and the Top Ten List.

person.name: Jay Leno
person.fact: He hosted The Tonight Show for 22 years and is as famous for his passion for collecting hundreds of vintage cars and motorcycles as he is for his massive chin.

person.name: Johnny Carson
person.fact: He defined late-night television for 30 years as host of The Tonight Show, and his endorsement of a little-known stand-up on air was considered a career-making moment for comedians.

person.name: Jon Stewart
person.fact: His satirical Daily Show became the primary source of news for millions of young Americans and is credited with influencing real political coverage during the Bush and Obama eras.

person.name: John Oliver
person.fact: His long-form investigative comedy segments on Last Week Tonight have repeatedly caused real-world consequences, including crashing the FCC's website during his net neutrality piece.

person.name: Trevor Noah
person.fact: The South African comedian took over The Daily Show from Jon Stewart and wrote a bestselling memoir, Born a Crime, about growing up mixed-race under apartheid.

person.name: Larry King
person.fact: He conducted over 50,000 interviews across his career, wearing his signature suspenders every night on CNN's Larry King Live for 25 years.

person.name: Barbara Walters
person.fact: She was the first female co-anchor of a network evening news program and created The View, a daytime panel show that became a cultural institution for political and social debate.

person.name: Anderson Cooper
person.fact: The silver-haired CNN anchor is the son of Gloria Vanderbilt and became a household name for his on-the-ground hurricane and war coverage, including his emotional reporting from post-Katrina New Orleans.

person.name: Walter Cronkite
person.fact: Known as 'the most trusted man in America,' his on-air emotional reaction to JFK's assassination and his editorial declaring the Vietnam War a stalemate helped shape public opinion.

person.name: Diane Sawyer
person.fact: A former Nixon White House aide turned award-winning journalist, she conducted some of television's most-watched interviews, including a 2015 sit-down with Caitlyn Jenner.

person.name: Katie Couric
person.fact: She became the first female solo anchor of a major U.S. network evening news broadcast when she joined CBS News in 2006, after 15 years as a beloved Today show host.

person.name: Tucker Carlson
person.fact: The bow-tie-wearing host became Fox News's most-watched primetime anchor before being abruptly fired in 2023, then relaunching independently on X (formerly Twitter).

person.name: Rachel Maddow
person.fact: Her MSNBC show became cable news's top-rated program, known for its long narrative-style cold opens that connected historical dots to current political events.

person.name: Dr. Phil
person.fact: Phil McGraw built a daytime TV empire dispensing tough-love advice after being discovered by Oprah Winfrey, but became controversial for his handling of mentally vulnerable guests.

person.name: Dr. Oz
person.fact: The celebrity heart surgeon became a daytime TV health guru through Oprah's endorsement, later running for U.S. Senate in Pennsylvania where his promotion of miracle cures became a major liability.

person.name: Judge Judy
person.fact: Judge Judy Sheindlin's no-nonsense courtroom show became the longest-running court program in TV history, and she negotiated a salary that made her the highest-paid TV personality for years.

person.name: Ryan Seacrest
person.fact: The seemingly omnipresent host ran American Idol and E! News simultaneously while also doing morning radio, building a multi-platform media empire before eventually taking over from Pat Sajak on Wheel of Fortune.

person.name: Simon Cowell
person.fact: The notoriously blunt music executive and TV judge created and produced American Idol, The X Factor, and Britain's Got Talent, launching the careers of artists including One Direction and Susan Boyle.

person.name: Howard Stern
person.fact: The self-proclaimed 'King of All Media' pushed the boundaries of broadcast radio for decades, earning record FCC fines, before moving to SiriusXM satellite radio in a $500 million deal.

person.name: Bob Ross
person.fact: The soft-spoken painter with the iconic afro taught millions to paint 'happy little trees' on his PBS show The Joy of Painting, filming over 400 episodes in 11 years and becoming a posthumous internet icon.

person.name: Mr. Rogers
person.fact: Fred Rogers testified before the U.S. Senate in 1969 in a quiet, sincere speech that convinced lawmakers to preserve $20 million in PBS funding, famously reciting the lyrics to 'What Do You Do with the Mad That You Feel.'

person.name: Steve Irwin
person.fact: The exuberant Australian 'Crocodile Hunter' brought wildlife conservation to millions through his fearless hands-on encounters with dangerous animals, before dying tragically from a stingray barb in 2006.

person.name: Anthony Bourdain
person.fact: The former New York chef turned the travel food show into literary television with Parts Unknown, earning Peabody Awards for episodes that used food as a lens into geopolitics and human stories.

person.name: Jerry Seinfeld
person.fact: He co-created and starred in Seinfeld, often called the greatest sitcom ever made, built around observational comedy about nothing, and famously turned down $5 million per episode to end the show on his own terms.

person.name: Chris Rock
person.fact: His 1996 special Bring the Pain is considered one of the greatest stand-up specials ever recorded, and his commentary on race in America made him one of comedy's sharpest social critics.

person.name: Dave Chappelle
person.fact: He famously walked away from a $50 million Comedy Central deal in 2005 at the height of Chappelle's Show's popularity, later returning with Netflix specials that reignited fierce cultural debate.

person.name: Kevin Hart
person.fact: He sold out Madison Square Garden multiple times and became one of Hollywood's highest-paid actors, building a comedy-to-film crossover career rivaled by few stand-ups in history.

person.name: Eddie Izzard
person.fact: The British comedian pioneered a freewheeling, history-obsessed rambling style, often performing in heels and makeup, including a famous hour-long bit about the British Empire and Darth Vader's cafeteria.

person.name: Ricky Gervais
person.fact: He created the original British version of The Office, widely considered one of the greatest sitcoms ever made, and became known for his fearlessly provocative Golden Globes hosting appearances.

person.name: Sarah Silverman
person.fact: Her deliberately offensive and taboo-breaking comedy style made her one of the most polarizing voices in stand-up, and her 2005 mockumentary Jesus Is Magic blended music and comedy in an unusual format.

person.name: Amy Schumer
person.fact: Her sketch series Inside Amy Schumer won a Peabody Award and she became one of comedy's biggest names after her film Trainwreck, co-written with Judd Apatow, was a surprise box-office hit.

person.name: George Carlin
person.fact: His 'Seven Words You Can Never Say on Television' routine led to a landmark 1978 Supreme Court case that defined the FCC's power to regulate broadcast content.

person.name: Richard Pryor
person.fact: His brutally autobiographical comedy about race, addiction, and his own near-fatal self-immolation set the template for confessional stand-up and influenced virtually every comedian who followed him.

person.name: Bill Cosby
person.fact: His clean family-friendly comedy made him one of America's most beloved entertainers for decades, before he was convicted of sexual assault in 2018 following accusations from dozens of women.

person.name: Joan Rivers
person.fact: The acid-tongued comedian broke glass ceilings as one of the first women to host a late-night talk show and reinvented herself multiple times, becoming famous for her red-carpet fashion commentary.

person.name: Bob Hope
person.fact: He entertained U.S. troops overseas for nearly 50 years from World War II through the Gulf War, and his 14 Academy Awards hosting stints remain a record.

person.name: Rodney Dangerfield
person.fact: His 'I don't get no respect' catchphrase and bulging-eyed delivery made him a comedy icon, but he didn't achieve mainstream fame until his mid-40s, making his comeback one of comedy's great late-bloomer stories.

person.name: Bo Burnham
person.fact: He started as a teenage YouTube comedian and evolved into a writer-director whose Netflix special Inside, filmed entirely alone during COVID-19 lockdown, won three Emmy Awards.

person.name: John Mulaney
person.fact: His meticulous, storytelling-heavy stand-up style drew comparisons to classic comedians, but his personal life became tabloid fodder when he entered rehab and left his wife in a very public 2021 spiral.

person.name: Pete Davidson
person.fact: The Saturday Night Live cast member became as famous for his high-profile celebrity relationships, including a brief engagement to Ariana Grande, as for his self-deprecating tattoo-covered persona.

person.name: Naomi Campbell
person.fact: One of the original supermodels and the first Black woman on the cover of French Vogue, she was also notorious for a series of phone-throwing incidents involving her personal assistants.

person.name: Cindy Crawford
person.fact: Her iconic mole and athletic physique made her one of the defining supermodels of the late 1980s and 1990s, and she appeared on over 500 magazine covers during her career.

person.name: Tyra Banks
person.fact: She was the first Black woman to appear on the covers of the Sports Illustrated Swimsuit Issue and the Victoria's Secret catalog, later creating and hosting America's Next Top Model.

person.name: Heidi Klum
person.fact: The German supermodel became a cultural fixture as the longtime host and producer of Project Runway and as a Victoria's Secret Angel, famous for her elaborate and theatrical Halloween costumes.

person.name: Kate Moss
person.fact: Her waif-like look in the early 1990s defined a controversial shift in fashion aesthetics and she famously appeared in Calvin Klein campaigns that became some of the most talked-about ads of the decade.

person.name: Gisele Bündchen
person.fact: At her peak she was the world's highest-paid model for 15 consecutive years and walked the final Victoria's Secret Fashion Show in 2018 in one of fashion's most celebrated farewell moments.

person.name: Bella Hadid
person.fact: Named 'Model of the Year' by industry bible Models.com multiple times, she wore the iconic spray-painted 'naked dress' at the 2016 Cannes Film Festival in one of fashion's most viral moments.

person.name: Gigi Hadid
person.fact: She became one of the most in-demand runway models of her generation while maintaining a massive social media presence, and walked in a record number of shows during a single Fashion Week season.

person.name: Kendall Jenner
person.fact: Transitioning from Keeping Up with the Kardashians to the highest-paid model in the world by 2018, she became the face of dozens of luxury brands, but a Pepsi ad appearance sparked one of the decade's biggest advertising controversies.

person.name: Karlie Kloss
person.fact: One of the most recognizable runway models of the 2010s, she leveraged her career into tech philanthropy by founding Kode with Klossy, a coding camp for young women.

person.name: Ralph Lauren
person.fact: Born Ralph Lifshitz in the Bronx, he built a global empire from a line of wide neckties and turned the polo shirt into an American status symbol, without ever having formally studied fashion design.

person.name: Calvin Klein
person.fact: His minimalist aesthetic and provocative advertising, featuring a 15-year-old Brooke Shields saying 'nothing comes between me and my Calvins': made him as famous for controversy as for design.

person.name: Tommy Hilfiger
person.fact: He turned preppy American sportswear into a global brand by being one of the first designers to embrace hip-hop culture in the 1990s, a crossover that transformed the brand's identity.

person.name: Donatella Versace
person.fact: She took over her brother Gianni's fashion house after his murder in 1997 and maintained its maximalist, rock-star glamour identity, becoming as recognizable a figure as the brand itself.

person.name: Gianni Versace
person.fact: The Italian designer who made fashion theatrical and sexy was murdered outside his Miami mansion in 1997 by serial killer Andrew Cunanan, in one of fashion's most shocking tragedies.

person.name: Giorgio Armani
person.fact: He dressed Richard Gere in American Gigolo in 1980 and is credited almost single-handedly with changing how men dressed in suits, removing the structure and making tailoring soft and unlined.

person.name: Karl Lagerfeld
person.fact: The ponytailed, fan-carrying creative director of Chanel for over 35 years, he also simultaneously helmed Fendi and his own label while producing a legendary volume of sketches and opinions.

person.name: Yves Saint Laurent
person.fact: He is credited with inventing the modern women's power suit, the Le Smoking tuxedo, in 1966, a garment so radical that a woman was once turned away from a Paris restaurant for wearing it.

person.name: Marc Jacobs
person.fact: He was fired from Perry Ellis after his 1993 'grunge collection' was deemed a commercial disaster, only for that same collection to later be recognized as one of the most influential in American fashion history.

person.name: Tom Ford
person.fact: He revived Gucci from near-bankruptcy in the 1990s with overtly sexual, glamorous designs, then built his own namesake brand spanning fashion, beauty, and eventually film directing.

person.name: Vera Wang
person.fact: A figure skater who failed to make the 1968 U.S. Olympic team, she pivoted to fashion and transformed the bridal industry in her 40s, becoming the definitive name in high-end wedding gowns.

person.name: Anna Wintour
person.fact: The icy, bob-haired editor-in-chief of Vogue has wielded more power over the fashion industry than perhaps any other individual for four decades, and inspired the character Miranda Priestly in The Devil Wears Prada.

person.name: Kim Kardashian
person.fact: A 2007 sex tape leak became the unlikely launchpad for a media empire spanning reality TV, a billion-dollar shapewear brand (SKIMS), and a serious push toward criminal justice reform advocacy.

person.name: Kourtney Kardashian
person.fact: The eldest Kardashian sister launched the lifestyle brand Poosh and made headlines when her lavish wedding to drummer Travis Barker in Italy became one of the most-covered celebrity events of 2022.

person.name: Khloé Kardashian
person.fact: She built a fitness-focused brand and starred in Revenge Body after her very public relationship troubles played out on television, becoming the Kardashian most associated with personal reinvention.

person.name: Kris Jenner
person.fact: The self-described 'momager' orchestrated the rise of the entire Kardashian-Jenner media empire, negotiating deals and producing Keeping Up with the Kardashians for 20 seasons.

person.name: Kylie Jenner
person.fact: She built Kylie Cosmetics largely through Instagram and Snapchat into a brand Forbes initially valued at nearly $1 billion, making her one of the youngest self-made billionaires, a label that later sparked significant controversy.

person.name: Caitlyn Jenner
person.fact: Won the 1976 Olympic decathlon as Bruce Jenner, widely celebrated as the world's greatest athlete, before later becoming a prominent transgender public figure.

person.name: Paris Hilton
person.fact: Her leaked 2003 home video and the reality show 'The Simple Life' made her one of the first modern celebrities famous largely for being famous.

person.name: Gordon Ramsay
person.fact: Holds three Michelin stars at his London restaurant and became a global TV phenomenon by screaming at amateur cooks on 'Hell's Kitchen' and 'MasterChef'.

person.name: Julia Child
person.fact: A former OSS spy who didn't learn to cook until her 30s, she brought French cuisine to American kitchens through her landmark 1961 book 'Mastering the Art of French Cooking'.

person.name: Wolfgang Puck
person.fact: Revolutionized upscale American dining in the 1980s with smoked-salmon pizza at Spago, and has catered the Governors Ball after the Oscars for decades.

person.name: Jamie Oliver
person.fact: A British chef who launched a real-world campaign to overhaul school lunch menus in the UK and US, taking on governments over children's nutrition.

person.name: Bobby Flay
person.fact: Built his empire around bold Southwestern flavors and became famous for staging surprise 'throwdowns' against hometown cooks on his Food Network show.

person.name: Rachael Ray
person.fact: Popularized the concept of the '30-Minute Meal' and built a media empire from a simple cooking segment, never having attended culinary school.

person.name: Guy Fieri
person.fact: Bleached spiky hair, backward sunglasses, and a '67 Camaro are his trademarks as the self-styled 'Mayor of Flavortown' on 'Diners, Drive-Ins and Dives'.

person.name: Salt Bae
person.fact: Turkish butcher Nusret Gökçe became a global meme in 2017 for his theatrical slow-motion salt sprinkle over a perfectly rested steak.

person.name: Stephen King
person.fact: Has published over 60 horror novels under his own name and the pseudonym Richard Bachman, with works like 'The Shining' and 'It' defining modern horror.

person.name: J.K. Rowling
person.fact: A single mother on welfare, she wrote the first Harry Potter novel in Edinburgh cafés and went on to sell over 500 million books worldwide.

person.name: Maya Angelou
person.fact: Her 1969 memoir 'I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings' was one of the first autobiographies by a Black American woman to reach a mainstream audience, and she recited a poem at Bill Clinton's inauguration.

person.name: Toni Morrison
person.fact: The first African American woman to win the Nobel Prize in Literature, known for 'Beloved', a ghost story rooted in the brutal reality of American slavery.

person.name: Margaret Atwood
person.fact: A Canadian writer whose 1985 dystopian novel 'The Handmaid's Tale' surged back onto bestseller lists and spawned a hit TV series after the 2016 US election.

person.name: Salman Rushdie
person.fact: His 1988 novel 'The Satanic Verses' led Iran's Ayatollah Khomeini to issue a fatwa calling for his death, forcing him into hiding for nearly a decade.

person.name: Neil Gaiman
person.fact: Blends mythology, folklore, and fantasy in works like 'American Gods' and 'Sandman', the groundbreaking DC Comics series that helped legitimize graphic novels as literature.

person.name: George R. R. Martin
person.fact: Created the brutal, sprawling fantasy world of Westeros in 'A Song of Ice and Fire', inspiring 'Game of Thrones' and making fans wait decades for the next book.

person.name: Dan Brown
person.fact: 'The Da Vinci Code' sparked worldwide controversy in 2003 by weaving conspiracy theories about the Catholic Church and the Holy Grail into a thriller that sold 80 million copies.

person.name: James Patterson
person.fact: Has had more New York Times #1 bestsellers than any other author in history and reportedly sells roughly one book every 23 minutes around the world.

person.name: John Grisham
person.fact: A former Mississippi defense attorney who wrote his first legal thriller 'A Time to Kill' before dawn each morning and went on to define the legal thriller genre.

person.name: Malcolm Gladwell
person.fact: Popularized ideas like the '10,000-hour rule' and the 'tipping point' in accessible, anecdote-driven books that made social science feel like a page-turner.

person.name: Roald Dahl
person.fact: A WWII RAF fighter pilot and spy turned beloved children's author, whose dark, subversive stories like 'Charlie and the Chocolate Factory' also hide a very adult edge.

person.name: Dr. Seuss
person.fact: Theodor Geisel wrote 'The Cat in the Hat' using a deliberately limited vocabulary of 236 words, on a bet that he couldn't write an engaging children's book with so few.

person.name: C.S. Lewis
person.fact: A lifelong atheist who converted to Christianity after late-night walks with J.R.R. Tolkien, and hid a complete Christian allegory inside the 'Chronicles of Narnia'.

person.name: Harper Lee
person.fact: Published only one novel in her lifetime, 'To Kill a Mockingbird', which won the Pulitzer Prize in 1961 and became one of the most assigned books in American schools.

person.name: F. Scott Fitzgerald
person.fact: The gilded parties, bootleg champagne, and doomed romance of 1920s Long Island he captured in 'The Great Gatsby' were drawn largely from his own turbulent life.

person.name: John Steinbeck
person.fact: His Depression-era novel 'The Grapes of Wrath' about Dust Bowl migrants was so politically explosive that it was burned in several California counties.

person.name: Ray Bradbury
person.fact: 'Fahrenheit 451', his 1953 novel about book-burning, was itself ironically censored in some school editions: passages about religion and alcohol were quietly removed.

person.name: Isaac Asimov
person.fact: Wrote or edited over 500 books spanning almost every major category in the Dewey Decimal System, and his 'Three Laws of Robotics' shaped how science fiction, and real AI ethics, thinks about machines.

person.name: H.G. Wells
person.fact: His 1898 novel 'The War of the Worlds' was so convincingly adapted as a 1938 radio broadcast by Orson Welles that it allegedly caused widespread panic across the US.

person.name: Jules Verne
person.fact: His 19th-century adventure novels predicted the submarine, the moon rocket, and videoconferencing with uncanny accuracy, earning him the title 'Father of Science Fiction'.

person.name: J.D. Salinger
person.fact: Published 'The Catcher in the Rye' in 1951 and then retreated into total seclusion in rural New Hampshire, refusing interviews and publishing nothing more for decades.

person.name: Kurt Vonnegut
person.fact: A survivor of the Allied firebombing of Dresden, sheltering in a slaughterhouse, he turned that trauma into 'Slaughterhouse-Five', one of the great antiwar novels.

person.name: Truman Capote
person.fact: His 1966 'Black and White Ball' at the Plaza Hotel was dubbed the 'Party of the Century', but his unfinished novel 'Answered Prayers' destroyed his high-society friendships forever.

person.name: Hunter S. Thompson
person.fact: Invented 'Gonzo journalism' by embedding himself so deeply in stories, riding with the Hells Angels, covering Nixon's 1972 campaign, that he became the story.

person.name: Aldous Huxley
person.fact: Wrote 'Brave New World' in just four months in 1931; he and George Orwell both died on the same day, November 22, 1963, overshadowed by JFK's assassination.

person.name: Sylvia Plath
person.fact: Her semi-autobiographical novel 'The Bell Jar' and her raw, confessional poetry redefined how personal anguish could be turned into literary art before her death at 30.

person.name: Andy Warhol
person.fact: Turned a Campbell's Soup can and Marilyn Monroe's face into high art, and his New York studio 'The Factory' became the epicenter of 1960s pop culture.

person.name: Jackson Pollock
person.fact: Pioneered 'action painting' by laying canvas on the floor and dripping, flinging, and pouring paint, a method Life magazine celebrated by asking 'Is he the greatest living painter in the US?'

person.name: Georgia O'Keeffe
person.fact: Spent decades painting the bleached skulls and vast desert landscapes of New Mexico, becoming the 'Mother of American Modernism' while largely shunning the New York art world.

person.name: Norman Rockwell
person.fact: Spent 47 years illustrating the cover of the Saturday Evening Post, creating an idealized portrait of American small-town life that became inseparable from US nostalgia.

person.name: Banksy
person.fact: An anonymous British street artist whose satirical stencils appear overnight on walls from London to Gaza, and whose painting famously shredded itself moments after selling at auction for £1 million.

person.name: Jean-Michel Basquiat
person.fact: Rose from a New York graffiti writer tagging under the name 'SAMO©' to sell paintings for millions before dying of a heroin overdose at just 27.

person.name: Roy Lichtenstein
person.fact: Borrowed the bold outlines, Ben-Day dots, and melodrama of comic strips to create monumental fine-art paintings that blurred the line between popular culture and high art.

person.name: Henri Matisse
person.fact: After illness confined him to a wheelchair in his 70s, he invented an entirely new medium, cutting and arranging painted paper, producing some of his most celebrated works.

person.name: Claude Monet
person.fact: Painted his famous water lily series while nearly blind from cataracts, and created an outdoor studio pond at Giverny that he tended as obsessively as a canvas.

person.name: Pierre-Auguste Renoir
person.fact: Continued painting almost until his death despite crippling arthritis that left him unable to hold a brush, reportedly having it tied to his hand.

person.name: Edgar Degas
person.fact: Obsessively painted and sculpted ballet dancers, producing over 600 works on the subject, despite never romanticizing the art form, showing the grueling labor behind the grace.

person.name: Paul Cézanne
person.fact: Worked in near isolation in Provence for decades, but his geometric approach to apples, mountains, and card players directly inspired Picasso and became the bridge to Cubism.

person.name: Edvard Munch
person.fact: His 1893 painting 'The Scream', inspired by a real moment of existential dread on a walk, has become one of the most recognizable images of modern anxiety.

person.name: Gustav Klimt
person.fact: His golden, ornate portrait 'Woman in Gold' was looted by Nazis and became the subject of a landmark legal battle won by the subject's niece over 60 years later.

person.name: Marcel Duchamp
person.fact: Submitted a factory-bought urinal titled 'Fountain' to an art exhibition in 1917, asking whether context alone makes something art, a question the art world still hasn't settled.

person.name: Yayoi Kusama
person.fact: A Japanese artist who has voluntarily lived in a psychiatric institution since 1977, continuing to create from there; her mirrored 'Infinity Rooms' now draw hours-long queues worldwide.

person.name: Ansel Adams
person.fact: His sweeping black-and-white photographs of Yosemite and the American West, often made on large-format cameras on treacherous terrain, helped secure the expansion of the national park system.

person.name: Annie Leibovitz
person.fact: Her Rolling Stone cover images, including the last photo of John Lennon hours before his murder, and her intimate Vanity Fair celebrity portraits defined decades of American pop culture photography.

person.name: Ferdinand Magellan
person.fact: Led the first expedition to circumnavigate the globe, though he was killed in the Philippines before completing the voyage, and only 18 of his original 270 crew members made it home.

person.name: Lewis and Clark
person.fact: Led the Corps of Discovery 8,000 miles across unmapped North America to the Pacific Ocean between 1804 and 1806, guided in large part by a teenage Shoshone woman.

person.name: Roald Amundsen
person.fact: Beat Robert Falcon Scott to the South Pole in December 1911 by using sled dogs efficiently and preparing obsessively, while Scott's rival British team perished on the return journey.

person.name: Ernest Shackleton
person.fact: When his ship 'Endurance' was crushed by Antarctic ice, he led all 27 of his crew to safety across hundreds of miles of open ocean in a lifeboat, losing not a single man.

person.name: Edmund Hillary
person.fact: On May 29, 1953, the New Zealand beekeeper became one of the first two confirmed humans to summit Everest; he learned of his knighthood while still on the mountain.

person.name: Tenzing Norgay
person.fact: The Sherpa mountaineer who summited Everest alongside Edmund Hillary declined to say who stepped onto the top first, calling it a private moment shared between friends.

person.name: Jacques Cousteau
person.fact: Co-invented the Aqua-Lung scuba device in 1943 and spent decades aboard his ship Calypso filming the underwater world, bringing the ocean into living rooms worldwide.

person.name: Evel Knievel
person.fact: His failed 1974 attempt to rocket across Snake River Canyon attracted more live TV viewers than the moon landing, despite ending in an inglorious splashdown.

person.name: Felix Baumgartner
person.fact: In 2012 he skydived from a capsule 24 miles above Earth, breaking the sound barrier in freefall and setting multiple world records before landing safely in the New Mexico desert.

person.name: George S. Patton
person.fact: Commanded the fastest armored advance in military history across France in 1944, and controversially slapped a shell-shocked soldier in a military hospital, nearly ending his career.

person.name: Douglas MacArthur
person.fact: Famously pledged 'I shall return' when forced out of the Philippines in 1942, waded ashore to fulfill the promise in 1944, and later accepted Japan's formal surrender on the USS Missouri.

person.name: Robert E. Lee
person.fact: Turned down command of the Union Army to instead lead the Confederate Army of Northern Virginia, believing his loyalty to Virginia outweighed his duty to the United States.

person.name: Stonewall Jackson
person.fact: Earned his nickname by standing firm 'like a stone wall' at the First Battle of Bull Run; he was accidentally shot by his own troops at Chancellorsville and died days later.

person.name: William Tecumseh Sherman
person.fact: His 'March to the Sea' through Georgia in 1864 deliberately destroyed civilian infrastructure to break the South's will to fight, pioneering the concept of total war.

person.name: Erwin Rommel
person.fact: So respected by both sides in North Africa that Churchill praised him in Parliament, and so feared that Allied commanders pinned his portrait on their walls as a motivational reminder.

person.name: Al Capone
person.fact: Ran Chicago's criminal empire during Prohibition but was ultimately brought down not by violence charges, but by a federal tax evasion conviction in 1931.

person.name: John Gotti
person.fact: Known as the 'Teflon Don' after three high-profile acquittals, he was finally convicted in 1992 largely because his underboss Sammy 'The Bull' Gravano turned government witness.

person.name: Pablo Escobar
person.fact: At his peak the Colombian cocaine kingpin was smuggling 15 tons of cocaine into the US per day and reportedly spending $2,500 a month just on rubber bands for his cash.

person.name: Bonnie and Clyde
person.fact: The Depression-era outlaw couple robbed banks and small stores across five states, becoming folk anti-heroes before being ambushed in a hail of 130 bullets on a Louisiana road in 1934.

person.name: John Dillinger
person.fact: Escaped from an 'escape-proof' Indiana jail using a wooden gun he'd whittled himself, before being betrayed by the 'Lady in Red' and shot outside a Chicago theater in 1934.

person.name: Jesse James
person.fact: A former Confederate guerrilla who became America's most romanticized outlaw, he was shot in the back of the head by a fellow gang member hoping to collect a reward.

person.name: Billy the Kid
