person.fact: The New Mexico outlaw reportedly killed 21 men by his 21st birthday, historians put the real number closer to 8, and was shot dead by Sheriff Pat Garrett in a darkened room.

person.name: Ted Bundy
person.fact: Charming and articulate enough to act as his own attorney, he confessed to 30 murders across seven states shortly before his 1989 execution, to the end fascinated by his own case.

person.name: Charles Manson
person.fact: Never personally committed the 1969 Tate-LaBianca murders himself but orchestrated them through his 'Family' cult, believing the killings would trigger a race war he called 'Helter Skelter'.

person.name: Jeffrey Dahmer
person.fact: A Milwaukee factory worker who murdered and dismembered 17 men and boys between 1978 and 1991; one victim escaped and led police to the apartment where his crimes were finally uncovered.

person.name: Confucius
person.fact: A wandering Chinese scholar who spent 13 years traveling from court to court trying unsuccessfully to get rulers to adopt his ideas; his teachings became the foundation of Chinese society only after his death.

person.name: Sun Tzu
person.fact: The ancient Chinese general's 'The Art of War', written around 500 BC, is still required reading at military academies and business schools worldwide.

person.name: Buddha
person.fact: Born a sheltered Hindu prince in Nepal, Siddhartha Gautama abandoned his palace and family at 29 to seek enlightenment, and attained it meditating beneath a fig tree.

person.name: Jesus Christ
person.fact: A carpenter's son from Galilee whose three-year public ministry, death by crucifixion, and reported resurrection became the founding events of the world's largest religion.

person.name: Muhammad
person.fact: The Prophet of Islam received the first Quranic revelations at age 40 in a cave outside Mecca, and within 23 years had united the warring tribes of Arabia under a new faith.

person.name: Moses
person.fact: According to the Bible, he parted the Red Sea to lead the Israelites out of Egyptian slavery, received the Ten Commandments on Mount Sinai, and yet never entered the Promised Land himself.

person.name: Socrates
person.fact: Never wrote anything down; everything we know about him comes through his students, and his method of relentless questioning so irritated Athens that he was tried and forced to drink hemlock.

person.name: Charlemagne
person.fact: United most of Western Europe for the first time since Rome, was crowned Holy Roman Emperor on Christmas Day 800 AD, and is considered the founding father of both France and Germany.

person.name: King Arthur
person.fact: The legendary British king whose court at Camelot, the Knights of the Round Table, and the quest for the Holy Grail have inspired literature and film for over a thousand years.

person.name: Vlad the Impaler
person.fact: The 15th-century Wallachian prince who impaled tens of thousands of enemies on stakes was likely the historical inspiration for Bram Stoker's Count Dracula.

person.name: Henry VIII
person.fact: Broke with the Catholic Church and established the Church of England primarily so he could divorce his first wife, eventually having six wives in total: two of whom he beheaded.

person.name: Louis XIV
person.fact: Ruled France for 72 years, the longest reign of any major European monarch, and reportedly declared 'L'état, c'est moi' ('I am the state'), centralizing all power in himself.

person.name: Tutankhamun
person.fact: The boy pharaoh died at around 19 and was largely forgotten for 3,000 years until Howard Carter opened his nearly intact tomb in 1922, triggering a worldwide obsession with ancient Egypt.

person.name: Ramesses II
person.fact: Ruled Egypt for 66 years, built more monuments and fathered more children (over 100) than any other pharaoh, and had his name carved so deeply into existing temples that successors couldn't erase it.

person.name: Nefertiti
person.fact: The Egyptian queen whose 1912 limestone bust is one of the most copied artworks of antiquity; she may have briefly ruled Egypt as pharaoh in her own right after Akhenaten's death.

person.name: Hammurabi
person.fact: The Babylonian king who inscribed one of the world's first written legal codes on a seven-foot stone column around 1754 BC, including the famous 'eye for an eye' principle.

person.name: Hernán Cortés
person.fact: With just 500 men, horses, and steel weapons, he toppled the Aztec Empire: aided decisively by smallpox and by alliances with peoples who resented Aztec rule.

person.name: Francisco Pizarro
person.fact: An illiterate Spanish pig farmer who led the conquest of the Inca Empire, capturing Emperor Atahualpa in 1532 and demanding a ransom of gold that filled an entire room.

person.name: Davy Crockett
person.fact: A Tennessee frontiersman and congressman who became a legend through his own tall tales, dying at the Alamo in 1836 and becoming the most mythologized figure of the American frontier.

person.name: Daniel Boone
person.fact: Blazed the Wilderness Road through the Cumberland Gap, founding Boonesborough in Kentucky, and was captured twice by Shawnee warriors: one time being adopted into the tribe.

person.name: Sitting Bull
person.fact: The Hunkpapa Lakota chief who united the Sioux and Cheyenne nations at the Battle of Little Bighorn in 1876, where they annihilated Custer's Seventh Cavalry.

person.name: Crazy Horse
person.fact: Never allowed his photograph to be taken and refused to live on a reservation; killed in a disputed incident at a US Army fort in 1877, he is now the subject of the world's largest mountain sculpture.

person.name: Geronimo
person.fact: The Apache leader who outmaneuvered thousands of US and Mexican troops for decades before surrendering in 1886; his name became a battle cry for American paratroopers in WWII.

person.name: Pocahontas
person.fact: The Powhatan chief's daughter who helped broker an uneasy peace between her people and Jamestown's English settlers, was later captured, converted to Christianity, and died in England aged around 21.

person.name: Sacagawea
person.fact: The Shoshone teenager who joined the Lewis and Clark expedition as an interpreter while carrying a newborn, and whose presence signaled to other tribes that the expedition was peaceful.

person.name: Helen Keller
person.fact: Left deaf and blind by illness at 19 months, she learned to read, write, and speak through the extraordinary teaching of Anne Sullivan, and went on to become a published author and political activist.

person.name: John Wayne
person.fact: The quintessential Hollywood cowboy who starred in over 170 films, won an Oscar for 'True Grit', and became such a symbol of American masculinity that the US Congress awarded him the Congressional Gold Medal.

person.name: Gene Kelly
person.fact: Choreographed and performed the legendary 'Singin' in the Rain' sequence in one take while running a high fever, redefining what dance could look like on film.

person.name: Fred Astaire
person.fact: His partnership with Ginger Rogers in 1930s Hollywood musicals was so perfectly matched that it was said she did everything he did but backwards and in high heels.

person.name: Ginger Rogers
person.fact: Won the Academy Award for Best Actress for 'Kitty Foyle', a dramatic role, proving she was far more than Fred Astaire's dancing partner, though their ten films together remain iconic.

person.name: Jane Fonda
person.fact: Won two Oscars for acting, launched a fitness video empire in the 1980s, and earned the lasting nickname 'Hanoi Jane' for a controversial visit to North Vietnam during the war.

person.name: The Undertaker
person.fact: The WWE's legendary 'Dead Man' character, played by Mark Calaway, went undefeated at WrestleMania for 21 years, a streak so iconic that its ending in 2014 left fans visibly in shock.

person.name: Andre the Giant
person.fact: Stood 7'4" and weighed over 500 pounds, went undefeated in wrestling for 15 years, could reportedly drink 100 beers in a single sitting, and was immortalized in the 'OBEY' street art poster.

person.name: Ric Flair
person.fact: 'To be the man, you gotta beat the man': the 'Nature Boy' won a record 16 world heavyweight championships and his sequined robes, 'WOO!' catchphrase, and figure-four leglock defined professional wrestling for four decades.
